edited_text:
About a year ago I self-published a nonfiction book about my attempt to seriously improve at a niche sport. It was a fun project, part memoir and part practical guide, and I was honestly expecting a small but enthusiastic audience.


In one sense, it “worked.” The book hit #1 in its Amazon category a few times and has stayed near the top of some very specific bestseller lists. I’ve had nice emails from readers, a few blog mentions, and some encouraging reviews. From the outside, it probably looks like a little success.


Financially, though, the results have been a bit disappointing. Across paperback, ebook, and audio, it has made only a few thousand dollars in a year. I’m grateful for that, but it was far from justifying the time, looking at it from a business perspective.


I’m curious how other nonfiction writers think about this. Is niche authority enough, or is the real challenge finding a market that is actually willing to buy?

I don’t really have anyone in my day-to-day life who understands why this feels so significant, so I’m sharing it here: I passed 20,000 words on my draft tonight.

For years, I was “writing a novel” in the sense that I had a folder full of openings, character notes, maps, and anxious rewrites of chapter one. Every sentence had to justify itself before I could move on. If a scene felt weak, I’d stop and try to solve the entire book at once. Unsurprisingly, I never got very far.

A few months ago, I made myself a simple rule: write 300 words a day, even if they were ugly, even if I knew I’d have to cut them later. At first, it felt almost physically uncomfortable to leave problems unsolved. But now the story actually exists beyond the idea of it, and that’s new for me.

It’s still messy, but it’s mine, and I’m finally moving forward.

I just typed the final sentence of what I am finally able to call the finished version of my novel, and now I don’t know what to do with myself.

I have been working on this for years. It has survived false starts, entire chapters getting thrown out, characters changing their minds about who they were, and more “final” drafts than I want to admit. At one point I was convinced a certain subplot was the heart of the book, and now it’s completely gone. Meanwhile, a tiny scene I added just to bridge two chapters somehow became one of my favorites.

The strangest part is looking back at the earliest pages and recognizing the story, but barely recognizing the writer. I can see all the clumsy choices I had to make before I learned how to make better ones.

I know there’s still querying, rejection, editing, and a long road ahead. But tonight, I’m letting myself celebrate: I finished the book!

I received a call today about an offer on my book. I am not saying much because nothing is signed or official and I'm definitely not trying to jinx it. 

I've been pacing around my kitchen acting as if my dishwasher completely understood what was going on, but I just needed to vent somehow so this was the way to do it. In the meantime, my agent is handling all the steps and hopefully I'll have the details soon so I can let everyone including family, co-workers and the group chat know. 

After writing five books, this one was the hardest as I almost gave up trying twice. The first two were useful in terms of learning about gravity, the third was close to that idea and patience was learned from the fourth. 

There's still a lot of things to do such as paperwork, more edits and the waiting game ahead. Tonight, I'm just going to be happy because it all finally happened - it actually happened.

I used to write constantly. Bad fantasy scenes, weird little horror pieces, pages of dialogue that went nowhere—none of it was “good,” probably, but it felt alive. Then I took writing seriously. Workshops, craft books, critique groups, all of it.

Now, every time I sit down, I cannot get past the first paragraph without interrogating it to death. Is the narrator distinct enough? Is the conflict clear? Is this image doing enough work? What does the setting represent? Am I relying on cliché? Is the theme too obvious, or worse, not there at all?

I know these are useful questions eventually but they have moved into the drafting stage and started acting like bouncers. Nothing gets in unless it can prove its literary value at the door.

I miss writing before I knew what I was “supposed” to be doing. I do not want to be ignorant again. I just want the joy back.

I made a small, free to use writing app because I kept having the same fight with my own drafts. I would sit down to write a chapter and somehow spend forty minutes polishing one sentence, checking a “quick” fact, opening another browser tab, or convincing myself I needed the perfect name for a side character before I could continue. This lack of focus and flow was getting me nowhere.


The app is meant to be a deliberately uncomfortable first-draft space. It strips away formatting, blocks the usual escape routes and distractions, makes it difficult to keep reaching back into last week's paragraph instead of writing today’s page. The whole point is momentum over elegance. You can fix ugly later but you can’t revise a blank document.


I’m not claiming this will work for everyone, and it definitely is not a full replacement for a serious editing program. It’s just a tool for getting to the end when your inner editor keeps grabbing the wheel. If that sounds familiar, I hope it helps.

This is going to sound a little ridiculous, but I accidentally found a really useful plotting exercise by pretending my own book already had an obsessive fandom.

I took a scene that felt pretty straightforward and asked myself, “If people online were trying to prove this meant something else, what would they point to?” Then I started doing it with character motives, storyline details, unanswered questions, all of it. Instead of treating my outline as the author, I treated it like an outsider.

And somehow it worked. A side character’s odd choices suddenly suggested a much better hidden agenda. A couple of scenes I thought were just connective tissue became potential foreshadowing. Even places where the story felt too clean gave me chances to add tension without changing the actual events.

I’m not saying every story needs a giant secret conspiracy, but looking at my draft through the eyes of a suspicious reader made it feel less flat. If your plot seems too simple, try misinterpreting it on purpose.

For the long time I thought “being a writer” meant making progress on the novel I’d been carrying around in my head since college. It had maps, religions, political factions, three magic systems, and absolutely no finished chapters. Every time I opened the document, I felt overwhelmed.

Then, almost by accident, I started writing little stories for a multiplayer game my friends and I were obsessed with. The official lore was ridiculous, a bit contradictory, and clearly not meant to be anything serious, which turned out to be the best possible thing for me. There was no pressure to be profound. I could write a scene in one evening, post it, make people laugh, and move on. 

It taught me that finishing small, imperfect things builds muscles that planning a masterpiece never did. I still care about the big project, but now I’m not trying to approach it like a sacred object. I’m just trying to write and find the joy again.

I just typed “The End” on the first draft of my first novel, and I'm staring at the screen like it might disappear if I blink.


I’m fifteen, and I started thislast year because I had a cool idea. It’s a fantasy/sci-fi mess with too many dramatic speeches, a villain who changes motivations every hundred pages, and at least three plot twists I’m pretty sure make no sense.


But it’s finished. An actual beginning, middle, and end. Around 90,000 words of probably terrible writing that I didn't want to give up on.

The next step is editing, but I’m not sure what to look for first, or whether I should show it to anyone yet. For people who’ve finished a first draft: what did you do next?

I don’t really have anyone in my day-to-day life who understands why this feels so huge. So I’m putting it here.

For the last couple of years, my manuscript has basically been an icon on my desktop that made me feel guilty whenever I noticed it. I’d open it once in a while, read a paragraph, decide that everything was terrible, and close it again. Work, school, bills, relationships, and general burnout all became very convenient reasons not to try.


On Monday night I opened it for “just ten minutes,” mostly to prove to myself that I didn’t care anymore. Instead, I found myself remembering why I started it. I fixed one scene, wrote another, and then stayed up way too late.

It’s now Thursday, and I’ve added a little over 18,000 words.

I know momentum like this can vanish, and I’m trying not to treat it like magic or a cure-all. But for the first time in ages, writing feels alive again, instead of like homework from a past version of myself.

Online shopping motivation refers to the reasons people choose to search for, compare, and buy products through digital platforms rather than in physical stores. In plain language, it is about what pushes or pulls a person toward buying online. Some motivations are practical, such as saving time, finding lower prices, or accessing products that are not available locally. Others are emotional, such as feeling excited by a sale, comforted by shopping from home, or rewarded by receiving a package. Online shopping is therefore not only a matter of convenience; it is also connected to how people regulate their mood, make decisions, learn from experiences, and respond to digital environments.


Motivation plays a central role because online shopping can satisfy both needs and wants. A person may shop online because they need a replacement phone charger quickly, but they may also browse clothing websites because they are bored, stressed, or seeking pleasure. This shows the difference between goal-directed and emotion-driven motivation. Goal-directed shopping is usually planned and purposeful. The shopper needs something and uses online tools to solve it efficiently. Emotion-driven shopping may be less planned. The act of browsing, imagining ownership, adding items to a cart, or finding discounts can create enjoyment before any purchase is actually made. Motivations can vary, but online shopping can serve both goal-directed and emotion-driven shopping.


Online shopping also affects decision-making. Digital stores are designed to reduce effort and encourage action. Search filters, customer reviews, personalized recommendations, free shipping thresholds, countdown timers, and “only a few left” messages all influence how people evaluate choices. These features can help consumers make informed decisions, but they can also create pressure. For example, a limited-time discount may make someone feel that delaying a purchase means losing an opportunity. In this way, emotion and motivation interact: excitement, fear of missing out, and anticipated regret can speed up decisions that might otherwise require more thought.


Learning is also involved. Consumers gradually learn which websites they trust, which brands fit their needs, and which cues signal quality or risk. A positive experience, such as fast delivery and an easy return process, can reinforce future online shopping. A negative experience, such as receiving a poor-quality item or dealing with hidden fees, can reduce motivation to buy from that seller again. Over time, shoppers develop habits. Checking prices online before buying, reading reviews, waiting for sales, or using wish lists can become routine behaviors shaped by past outcomes.


A concrete example is a university student buying a laptop online before the semester begins. The student is motivated by a practical need: they require a reliable device for lectures, assignments, and exams. They compare models, read reviews, watch video demonstrations, and look for student discounts. At the same time, emotions influence the process. They may feel anxious about spending a large amount of money, excited about getting a new device, and reassured by positive reviews. If the website offers a clear warranty, fast shipping, and a simple return policy, the student may feel more confident and complete the purchase. This example shows how online shopping motivation includes usefulness, trust, emotion, and decision-making all at once.


This topic matters because online shopping is now part of everyday life for many people. Understanding why people buy online can help consumers make more thoughtful choices and avoid impulsive spending. It can also help businesses design fairer and more user-friendly shopping experiences. More broadly, online shopping motivation reveals how technology shapes human behavior. Digital marketplaces do not simply provide products; they create environments that guide attention, trigger feelings, reward habits, and influence decisions. Recognizing these forces is important for promoting healthier consumption, better financial choices, and more ethical digital design.

Mindsets are the beliefs people hold about themselves, their abilities, and the meaning of success or failure. A mindset can be though of as the “story” a person tells themselves about what they can do and why outcomes happen. For example, a student might believe that they are simply “bad at maths,” or they might believe that maths is difficult now but can improve with practice. These two interpretations can lead to very different learning outcomes and change the way the student experiences emotions and makes choices.


A useful way to understand mindsets is through the difference between fixed and growth views of ability. A fixed mindset treats intelligence, talent, or personality as mostly unchangeable. From this perspective, effort can feel threatening because needing to try hard may seem like proof of low ability. Failure can also feel demotivating, as if one poor result reveals something permanent about oneself. A growth mindset, however, sees ability as something that can develop over time. This does not mean everyone can become equally skilled at everything, but it does mean improvement is possible by consistent effort, being persistent, seeking the right guidance and adapting oneself.


Mindsets are closely connected to motivation because they influence what people think is worth doing. If someone believes their ability is fixed, they may avoid difficult tasks to protect their self-image. They might choose easy work, give up quickly, or compare themselves constantly with others. Their motivation may depend heavily on looking competent. A growth mindset tends to support more resilient motivation. Challenges can be interpreted as opportunities to learn rather than as threats. Mistakes become information rather than evidence of failure. This can encourage persistence, curiosity, and a willingness to seek help.


Mindsets also shape emotion. A person with a fixed mindset may feel shame, anxiety, or defensiveness when they struggle, because struggle seems to signal inadequacy. A person with a growth mindset may still feel frustration or disappointment, but those emotions are more likely to be temporary and manageable. The emotional meaning of difficulty changes: it becomes part of the learning process rather than a final judgment. This emotional difference matters because feelings strongly affect attention, memory, and decision-making. When people are overwhelmed by fear of failure, they may focus more on avoiding embarrassment than on understanding the task.


A concrete example can be seen in a first-year university student receiving a low mark on an essay. With a fixed mindset, the student might think, “I am not academic enough for university,” and avoid reading the feedback because it feels painful. They may put less effort into the next assignment or consider dropping the course. With a growth mindset, the student might think, “This result shows that my argument and structure need work.” They may meet with a tutor, study examples of strong essays, revise their planning process, and improve over time. The same event can therefore lead either to withdrawal or to constructive action.


This topic matters because mindsets influence everyday choices in education, work, relationships, health, and personal development. They affect whether people take risks, respond to criticism, recover from setbacks, and pursue long-term goals. Understanding mindsets can help individuals become more aware of the assumptions guiding their behaviour. It can also help teachers, parents, managers, and peers create environments where effort, strategy, and improvement are valued. A growth-oriented approach does not remove difficulty or guarantee success, but it can make people more willing to engage with challenges and learn from them. Thus, mindset is a powerful factor in motivation, emotion, and the direction of human behaviour.

Anorexia nervosa is a serious eating disorder in which a person persistently restricts food intake, often alongside intense fear of weight gain and a distorted sense of body size or self-worth. Although it is commonly described in terms of food and weight, it is also deeply connected to emotion, motivation, learning, and decision-making. A person with anorexia may not simply be “choosing not to eat” in an ordinary sense. Their behavior is shaped by powerful internal distress, social pressure, habits or beliefs about control, and rewards or punishments that can make restrictive eating feel meaningful or necessary.

Extrinsic motivation refers to behavior driven by external outcomes, such as praise, approval, status, avoidance of criticism, or gaining privileges. In anorexia nervosa, extrinsic motivation can play a role in both the development and treatment of the disorder. For example, a young person may initially lose weight and receive compliments from peers or family members. These comments may be intended kindly, but they can become powerful rewards. The person learns that weight loss brings attention, admiration, or a sense of achievement. Over time, eating less may become linked with feeling valued, disciplined, or accepted. This does not mean anorexia is caused only by outside approval, but external reinforcement can strengthen dangerous patterns.

Emotion is also central. Restricting food can temporarily reduce anxiety or create a feeling of control when other parts of life feel uncertain. If eating produces guilt, fear, or shame, then avoiding food may feel emotionally relieving. This relief functions like a reward, even though it harms the body. Decision-making can become increasingly rigid: the person may judge choices not by health or enjoyment, but by rules about calories, weight, exercise, or approval. As malnutrition worsens, thinking often becomes more inflexible, making recovery harder.

Extrinsic motivation is also used in treatment, especially when health risks are severe. Structured programs may offer privileges, increased independence, or social rewards when a person meets nutritional goals or gains weight safely. For instance, a hospital patient might earn permission to take short supervised walks or have more time with visitors after completing meals consistently. This approach can be useful because it encourages life-saving behavior before the person fully feels internally motivated to recover. However, it must be handled carefully. If treatment relies only on external rewards, the person may comply while supervised but struggle later. Long-term recovery usually requires a shift toward more internal reasons for change, like relationships, freedom, education, emotional stability, or a future not controlled by the disorder.

A concrete example is a university student who begins dieting after classmates praise her appearance. The praise makes restriction feel successful, and she starts linking thinness with social acceptance. When stress from study increases, eating less also gives her a sense of control. Eventually, she avoids meals, loses concentration, and withdraws from friends. In therapy, external supports may help her rebuild eating habits at first, but recovery deepens when she identifies personal reasons to get well, such as returning to sport for enjoyment rather than calorie burning, thinking clearly in classes, and reconnecting with people she cares about.

This topic matters because misunderstandings about anorexia can lead to blame or oversimplified treatment. Recognising the role of extrinsic motivation helps explain why harmful behaviors can become reinforced and why recovery often requires more than just telling someone to eat. It also highlights the responsibility of families, schools, media, and health professionals to avoid rewarding extreme thinness and to support healthier sources of self-worth. Understanding motivation allows treatment to be more compassionate, realistic, and effective.

The broaden-and-build theory of positive emotions proposes that pleasant emotional states do more than simply make people feel good in the moment. Emotions such as joy, interest, gratitude, amusement, hope, and love can widen a person’s attention. They can also make a broader range of thoughts and actions seem possible. When people feel safe, curious, or uplifted, they are more likely to explore, play, connect with others, try new strategies, and notice opportunities they might otherwise miss. Over time, these momentary expansions can build lasting resources, such as knowledge, confidence, friendships, coping skills, creativity, and physical resilience.


This idea is useful because it challenges the assumption that positive emotion is just a reward that comes after success. Instead, positive emotion can help create the conditions for success. A student who feels interested in a topic is more likely to ask questions, read beyond the minimum requirements, and make connections between ideas. A person who feels supported by friends may be more willing to take on a difficult challenge. Someone who feels hopeful during a setback may be better able to consider alternative solutions rather than giving up immediately. In this way, positive emotions are linked to motivation because they can increase approach behavior. People become more inclined to move toward tasks, people, and goals rather than withdraw from them.


The theory also helps explain how emotion affects learning and decision-making. Negative emotions such as fear or anger can narrow attention. This can be useful when there is a direct threat and fast action is needed. However, that same narrowing can limit flexible thinking when a problem requires creativity or reflection. Positive emotions often have the opposite effect. They can encourage mental flexibility. This makes it easier to see multiple options, combine information in new ways, and consider long-term benefits. This does not mean that positive feelings always lead to good decisions, or that negative emotions are always harmful. Instead it suggests that a balanced emotional life, with enough positive emotion to support openness and resilience, can improve how people learn and respond to challenges.


A concrete example can be seen in a first-year university student who is anxious about joining a group project. If the student feels isolated and tense, they may contribute only the minimum, such as avoiding speaking in meetings, and interpreting feedback as criticism. However, if the group creates a friendly atmosphere and the student experiences moments of enjoyment and belonging, their behavior may change. They might suggest an original idea, volunteer to research an unfamiliar topic, or ask a classmate for help. These small actions can build skills and relationships that last beyond the project itself. The positive emotion does not complete the assignment for the student, but it makes exploration and engagement more likely.


The broaden-and-build theory matters because it gives practical value to emotional wellbeing. It suggests that positive emotions are not superficial extras. They are important ingredients in human development. In education, workplaces, families, and communities, environments that foster curiosity, appreciation, humor, and connection can help people become more adaptable and capable. This is especially important in a world where people often face uncertainty, pressure, and change. Positive emotions cannot remove all difficulties. They can, however, expand the ways people respond to difficulties by helping them think more openly, act more creatively, and build stronger personal resources. Positive emotion plays a meaningful role in motivation, learning, behavior, and long-term flourishing.

Long-term goal achievement refers to the process of working toward an outcome that requires sustained effort over weeks, months, or years. Unlike a short-term goal, such as finishing a homework task tonight, a long-term goal involves persistence through changing circumstances, distractions, setbacks, and periods of low motivation. In plain language, it is the ability to keep moving toward something important even when the reward is far away.

Motivation is central to long-term goal achievement because people need a reason to continue when progress is slow. Some motivation comes from external rewards, such as earning a qualification, getting a job, or gaining recognition. However, long-term goals are often easier to maintain when they are also connected to internal reasons, such as personal values, curiosity, identity, or a sense of purpose. A student who wants to become a nurse may be motivated by the future career, but also by the belief that caring for others is meaningful. This deeper motivation can help sustain effort when the work becomes difficult.

Emotion also plays an important role. Long-term goals often involve frustration, boredom, doubt, and anxiety, especially when improvement is not obvious. People may begin with excitement, but that feeling usually fades. Successful goal achievement therefore depends not only on motivation, but also on managing emotions. This might involve tolerating temporary discomfort, recovering from disappointment, or finding encouragement after failure. Positive emotions matter too. Pride after small accomplishments, hope about future success, and satisfaction from consistent effort can reinforce commitment.

Learning and behavior are closely connected to long-term goals because achievement requires building skills and habits over time. A person rarely reaches a major goal through one dramatic effort. More often, progress comes from repeated actions that become part of daily life. For example, someone who wants to run a marathon does not simply decide to run 42 kilometres one day. They begin with shorter distances, follow a training schedule, learn how to pace themselves, adjust their diet and sleep, and gradually increase their endurance. Along the way, they may miss training sessions, experience muscle soreness, or feel discouraged. The goal becomes achievable because it is broken into smaller steps and supported by consistent routines.

Prioritization is also important because long-term goals require trade-offs. Choosing to study for a degree may mean giving up some leisure time, spending money on education, or delaying other opportunities. Each small decision either supports or weakens the larger goal. This is why planning matters. Clear goals help people decide what to prioritize, while vague goals are easier to abandon. Saying “I want to be healthier” may be inspiring, but saying “I will walk for 30 minutes after work four days a week” gives behavior a clear direction.

Long-term goal achievement matters because many of the most meaningful outcomes in life depend on it. Education, career development, physical health, financial stability, creative skill, and strong relationships all require effort over time. Understanding how long-term goals work helps people become more realistic and compassionate toward themselves. It shows that success is not simply a matter of willpower or talent. It often depends on setting clear aims, building supportive habits, managing emotions, learning from setbacks, and staying connected to a meaningful reason for continuing. In this way, long-term goal achievement is not just about reaching a future result; it is also about shaping the kind of person one becomes through sustained commitment.

Motivational interviewing helps people process change in a way that is not condescending. It is a way of speaking that helps a person see that change is normal. Motivational interviewing focuses on the variety of feelings that someone can have instead of on modifying their behaviour. For example, a person may want to stop smoking because they worry about their health, even though they enjoy smoking as a way to manage stress. They also may want to study more, but might have a fear of failure. Motivational interviewing works with this ambivalence rather than treating it as stubbornness.


The central idea is that lasting change is more likely when motivation comes from within the person. Advice can be useful, but advice alone often fails when it clashes with a person’s emotions, habits, identity, or sense of control. If someone feels judged, they may be more defensive of their behavior. Motivational interviewing reduces this defensiveness by creating a conversation in which the person feels heard. The helper asks open questions, listens carefully, reflects what the person is saying, and highlights the person’s values, strengths, and goals. This allows the person to hear their own arguments for change.


Motivational interviewing is closely connected to decision-making. Many important choices are not based on logic; but rather on fear, hope, guilt, pride, comfort, and uncertainty. A person deciding whether to change a behavior often weighs short-term rewards against long-term consequences. For example, eating unhealthy food may bring immediate pleasure and relief, while healthier eating may offer future benefits that feel less urgent. Motivational interviewing helps clear up competing motives. It invites people to consider questions such as: What do I want my life to look like? What is this behavior costing me? What would be different if I made a change? How confident do I feel, and what would increase that confidence?


It also connects to learning and behavior because lasting change is more than a decision. People need to learn new skills, practice different responses, and recover from setbacks. Motivational interviewing does not pretend that change is easy. Instead, it helps people identify small, realistic steps that fit their own circumstances. A person who wants to reduce alcohol use, for instance, may begin by noticing when and why they drink, choosing alcohol-free days, or planning what to say when friends offer drinks. These steps are more likely to succeed when the person sees them as self-chosen rather than imposed.


Another example is when a university student repeatedly misses morning classes. A direct approach might sound like, “You need to be more responsible and go to bed earlier.” This may be true, but it could make the student feel criticized. A motivational interviewing approach would sound different: “What do you like about staying up late, and what do you dislike about missing class?” The student might say that late nights are when they relax and connect with friends, but they are also worried about falling behind. The conversation could then explore how their current routine fits with their goal of passing the course. The student might decide to start by attending two key morning classes each week and setting a cut-off time for social media the night before.


This topic matters because many problems in health, education, relationships, and work involve behavior change. People are often told what they should do, yet information by itself does not always lead to action. Motivational interviewing offers a more humane and effective alternative. It respects autonomy, acknowledges emotional complexity, and helps people move toward change in a way that feels personally meaningful. At its best, it does not force motivation into someone; it helps them uncover motivation that is already there.

Affective forecasting refers to the way people predict how they will feel in the future. In everyday terms, it is emotional “weather forecasting”: we imagine how happy, disappointed, anxious, proud, or regretful we will be after something happens. A student might predict that receiving a high mark will make them happy for weeks, or that failing a test will be unbearable. A person choosing between jobs might imagine that a higher salary will bring lasting satisfaction while overlooking how daily stress, workplace culture, or commuting time could affect their mood.

These predictions matter because emotions are closely tied to motivation and decision-making. People often choose goals, avoid risks, persist with difficult tasks, or give up based on what they expect to feel later. If someone believes that exercising will feel unpleasant and unrewarding, they may avoid it, even if the actual experience leaves them feeling energised. If a student expects embarrassment from asking a question in class, they may stay silent and miss a learning opportunity. In this way, imagined future emotions can shape present behaviour almost as strongly as actual emotions.

A key problem is that people are not always accurate when predicting their future feelings. They may overestimate how intense an emotion will be or how long it will last. This can happen because attention becomes fixed on one aspect of a future event while other influences are ignored. For example, someone imagining a breakup may focus only on loneliness and pain without considering support from friends, new routines, or their own ability to adapt. Likewise, someone imagining the joy of buying an expensive item may focus on the excitement of ownership while underestimating how quickly the item will become ordinary.

Consider a university student deciding whether to give a presentation in a tutorial. The student predicts that speaking in front of others will be humiliating and that any mistake will bother them for days. Because of this forecast, they feel anxious before class and consider skipping the task. After giving the presentation, though, they may find that the anxiety fades quickly, classmates are supportive, and the experience brings a sense of achievement. Their original prediction was not entirely irrational because public speaking can be stressful, but it exaggerated the emotional cost and underestimated the possibility of relief and pride. This inaccurate forecast could have prevented learning, confidence building, and participation.

Affective forecasting is also important for understanding why people sometimes make choices that do not improve their wellbeing. Decisions about study, relationships, spending, health, and careers are often guided by assumptions about future happiness or distress. When those assumptions are inaccurate, people may chase goals that provide only brief satisfaction or avoid challenges that would help them grow. Greater awareness of affective forecasting can encourage more balanced decisions. Instead of asking only, “How do I think I will feel?” people can also ask, “Have I felt this way before?”, “How long did the feeling actually last?”, and “What other parts of my life will still be present after this event?”

This topic matters because it shows that human behaviour is influenced not only by current emotions but also by predictions about future emotions. Learning to question these predictions can improve motivation, resilience, and decision-making. It can help people take worthwhile risks, recover from setbacks, and make choices based on a more realistic understanding of how emotions change over time.

Public speaking anxiety is the fear people feel when they have to speak in front of others. It can range from mild nervousness to intense panic that makes one avoid presentations. In plain language, it is the feeling that being watched is threatening, even when there is no real danger. The body reacts as if the situation is unsafe. The heart may beat faster, breathing may be shallow, and organized thoughts may become difficult. At the same time, the person may worry about mistakes, looking foolish, forgetting things, or being evaluated.

This anxiety is connected to emotion because it involves fear and self-consciousness. These emotions are not simply “in the mind”; they influence the body. A student who feels anxious may read neutral facial expressions as signs of boredom. This can increase fear. Emotion also impacts attention. Instead of focusing on the message they want to communicate, an anxious speaker may focus on their hands, pauses, or failure. This can create a cycle wherein anxiety impacts them, and difficulties increase anxiety.

Public speaking anxiety also relates to motivation. Some people are motivated to prepare carefully because of this. In this way, a moderate level of nervousness can be useful. However, when anxiety becomes too strong, it can reduce motivation. A person may procrastinate or choose not to apply for leadership roles. These avoidance behaviors may bring short-term relief, but they often strengthen the fear over time.


Learning plays an important role in both the development and reduction of public speaking anxiety. A person who once had a humiliating experience while speaking may learn to associate presentations with danger. They may then expect future speaking situations to end badly, even if the original event was unusual. On the other hand, repeated manageable experiences can help reduce fear. If someone practices speaking in small groups, receives supportive feedback, and gradually works up to larger audiences, they can learn that anxiety is uncomfortable but not harmful. They may also learn practical skills, such as organizing ideas clearly, pausing instead of rushing, and making eye contact in a relaxed way.


For example, imagine a first-year university student who has to give a five-minute presentation in a tutorial. Before the presentation, she feels sick and considers skipping class. She worries that everyone will notice her nerves and think she is unprepared. Instead, she practices with a friend, writes brief cue cards, and reminds herself that the audience is mainly focused on understanding the topic, not judging her personally. During the presentation, her voice shakes at first, but she continues. By the end, she realizes that she was able to speak despite feeling anxious. This experience does not remove the fear completely, but it gives her evidence that she can manage it.


Public speaking anxiety matters because speaking to others is part of education, work, and civic life. Students present assignments, employees share ideas in meetings, and community members advocate for issues they care about. When anxiety prevents people from speaking, it can limit achievement, confidence, and participation. Understanding public speaking anxiety helps people respond with practical strategies rather than shame. It also reminds teachers, employers, and peers to create environments where people can build communication skills gradually and safely.

Grit is the capacity to keep working toward a meaningful long-term goal even when progress is slow, difficult, or discouraging. Simply put, it is  “sticking with it”; the goal matters enough to justify continued effort. Grit does not mean raw talent, momentary enthusiasm, or stubbornness in every situation. A gritty person is not someone who never feels bored, tired, or disappointed. Rather, grit involves continuing to focus on an aim despite these feelings, while learning from setbacks and adapting methods when necessary.

Grit is related to motivation because it requires both direction and endurance. Many people can feel motivated for a while, especially when a goal is new and exciting, but the harder challenge is maintaining effort after the novelty fades.  

Grit helps in moving from initial desire to sustained action, supported by intrinsic motivation from a person finding meaning in the goal. It can also involve external motives such as wanting to qualify for a profession, support a family, or contribute to a community. What matters is that the goal remains important enough to organise behaviour over time.

Emotion also plays a part. Long-term effort inevitably involves frustration, self-doubt, disappointment, and sometimes envy of people progressing faster. Grit does not remove these emotions, but changes how a person responds to them. Instead of interpreting failure as an invitation to quit, a gritty learner may see it as an indication of what needs more practice. This emotional regulation is important because giving up often occurs not when a task feels impossible or when the discomfort associated with it becomes too strong. Grit therefore involves accepting short term discomfort to reach a larger purpose.

In learning, grit can influence study habits, skill development, and academic achievement. A student who is gritty is more likely to revise after receiving poor feedback, attend practice sessions consistently, and seek help rather than avoid difficult content. A first-year university student who wants to become a nurse but struggles with anatomy might feel embarrassed after failing quiz and consider changing courses. Instead, she could review her mistakes, form a weekly study group, ask her tutor for guidance, and use diagrams and flashcards every evening. The improvement is not immediate, and she still may find the subject challenging, but over the course her understanding grows. In this case, grit is shown repeated, purposeful action after a setback, not by effortless success.

Grit also affects decision-making. People often face choices between short-term comfort and long-term benefit, for example, watching television or studying, sleeping in or attending training, avoiding feedback or confronting weaknesses. Grit helps align decisions with future goals rather than immediate moods. However, it should be balanced with flexibility. While persisting with a goal can be admirable, continuing at any cost may become harmful if the goal is unrealistic, damaging, or no longer meaningful. Wise grit includes knowing when to persist, when to change strategy, and when to redirect effort.

Many important outcomes in life require sustained effort rather than quick achievement. These include Education, careers, relationships, health, and personal growth, which all involve obstacles. Grit helps some people continue developing after early failure while others withdraw despite having ability. It also reminds educators, parents, and individuals that success is not only about talent but is also shaped by habits, purpose, emotional resilience, and the willingness to keep learning over time.

Stress recovery theory proposes that natural environments can help people recover from stress more quickly than many settings that are built or highly demanding. In plain language, the theory suggests that when people feel tense, overloaded, threatened, or emotionally drained, contact with nature can calm the body and mind. This does not mean that a park, garden, beach, or forest solves the problem that caused the stress. Rather, natural settings can reduce the intensity of stress reactions, making it easier for a person to think clearly, regulate emotions, and respond constructively.

A central idea is that stress is not only a mental experience but also a bodily one. When people feel under pressure, the body may shift into a state of alertness: the heart beats faster, muscles tighten, attention narrows, and negative emotions become stronger. These reactions can be useful in real danger, but they are exhausting when they continue for long periods. Stress recovery theory argues that many natural environments contain features that humans tend to experience as safe, pleasant, and gently engaging. Trees, water, open sky, birdsong, and soft patterns of light can draw attention without demanding intense effort. This can allow the nervous system to settle and helps negative emotions fade.

The theory is closely connected to emotion because it focuses on how environments influence mood and physiological arousal. A person who is stressed in a crowded train station or a noisy office may become irritable and impatient, not simply because of personal weakness, but because the setting keeps the body activated. By contrast, a quiet green space may support feelings of ease, interest, and restoration. These emotional shifts matter because emotions affect behavior. When people feel calmer, they often find it easier to listen, cooperate, solve problems, and resist impulsive reactions.

Stress recovery also connects with motivation and learning. High stress can reduce concentration, weaken memory, and make tasks feel more overwhelming than they really are. A student who is anxious before an exam may struggle to absorb information even after hours of study. If that student takes a short walk through a nearby garden or sits under trees between study sessions, the break may lower emotional tension and restore enough mental energy to continue. The natural setting does not replace studying, but it can make studying more effective by improving the student’s readiness to learn.

A concrete example is a hospital patient recovering from surgery. Imagine two patients with similar conditions. One spends most of the day looking at a blank wall and hearing constant corridor noise, while the other can see trees through a window and has access to a small courtyard. The second patient may still experience pain and worry, but the natural view and peaceful setting may help reduce stress, improve mood, and make the recovery period feel more manageable. This example shows why the theory has practical value in healthcare design, education, workplaces, and urban planning.

Stress recovery theory matters because modern life often places people in environments that are crowded, noisy, fast-paced, and mentally demanding. If surroundings can either intensify or ease stress, then wellbeing is not only an individual responsibility; it is also shaped by design and access. Green spaces, street trees, school gardens, and quiet outdoor areas are not luxuries. They can be part of a healthier social environment that supports emotional balance, better decision-making, and more sustainable behavior. Understanding stress recovery theory encourages people and communities to treat contact with nature as a meaningful resource for mental and physical health.

The paper addresses the problem of producing answers to long, non-factoid questions, with a focus on love advice questions from a Japanese community Q&A service. The proposed system has two main components: 

First, it learns word embeddings that are biased by document-level semantic information, such as question categories and title terms, with the goal of making words like “relationship” acquire different relevance in different topical contexts. 

Second, rather than selecting a whole existing answer, it constructs an answer by selecting and combining shorter sentence units of predefined types, such as conclusion and supplement. The model uses bidirectional LSTMs to represent questions and candidate answer sentences, a hinge-loss objective over correct and incorrect sentence combinations, and an attention mechanism from a selected conclusion sentence to a supplemental sentence. Experiments on Oshiete goo data suggest improvements over LSTM-based answer selection baselines, particularly in a harder answer construction setting, and the authors report deployment in a live love-advice service.


The paper tackles an interesting and practically relevant problem. Non-factoid Q&A is indeed an important setting where answer selection from a fixed archive is often insufficient, and the paper is right to emphasize that advice questions require sensitivity to situation, tone, and discourse structure. The attempt to move from whole answer retrieval to sentence level composition is a reasonable, intermediate step between retrieval and full neural generation. This is especially sensible for advice domains, where fully generative systems can produce fluent but unsafe, generic, or incoherent content. By constraining the output to human authored sentence fragments and a predefined rhetorical scenario, the system may offer a more controllable way to produce answers than unconstrained sequence generation.


A second strength is the paper’s attention to domain dependent word usage. Using category and title information as semantic context for representation learning is a simple but plausible idea, and it addresses a real weakness of generic embeddings in community Q&A data. The paper gives an intuitive motivation: certain words become important only in a particular question category, while others are common across many categories and should not dominate matching. The experimental comparison between the ordinary LSTM baseline and the semantic embedding variant suggests that this component contributes to the final performance.


The answer construction formulation is also appealing in its high-level design. The authors identify that answers in a domain such as love advice often have a stereotyped discourse structure, and they use this observation to decompose answer generation into selecting sentences for specific roles. This is a useful engineering insight. The model’s joint objective, which considers both question/sentence relevance and sentence/sentence compatibility, is preferable to independently selecting the best conclusion and best supplement. The inclusion of an attention mechanism from the conclusion to the supplement is also conceptually appropriate, since the supplemental sentence should elaborate on the same topic or rationale as the conclusion.


The practical orientation of the work is another positive aspect. The paper is based on a real Q&A service, uses a non-English dataset, and reports not only offline metrics but also some human judgments and deployment statistics. Many papers on Q&A rely on sanitized benchmark data and do not consider how a system behaves when used by real people. The fact that the authors attempted to deploy the model and collect user feedback makes the work potentially valuable to the community.


However, the paper has several significant weaknesses that limit its suitability for publication in its current form. The most important issue is that the claimed contribution as “answer generation” is overstated. The system does not generate novel sentences; it retrieves and recombines sentence fragments extracted from existing answers according to a manually specified scenario. This is a legitimate task, but it should be described more precisely as answer construction or sentence-level answer composition. The introduction repeatedly contrasts the method with answer selection and presents it as moving beyond selection, but the system is still fundamentally extractive. The paper would be stronger if it clearly defined the task as constrained extractive answer composition and discussed its limitations relative to genuine generation.


The evaluation methodology is not sufficiently convincing. The main offline construction metric measures whether the model can recreate original conclusion-supplement pairs. This is a very narrow proxy for answer quality. A constructed answer can be useful even if it does not match the original pair, and conversely an original pair may not be the only or best answer to a question. Average precision over exact or annotated original pairs therefore may underestimate or mischaracterize the task. At the same time, because many candidate sentences may be generic advice statements, the metric may also reward superficial matching. The paper needs a clearer definition of what counts as a correct constructed answer, how many gold combinations exist per question, and whether correctness was judged semantically or simply by identity with extracted sentences.


The human evaluation is promising but under described. The paper reports judgments by two experts, but it does not specify the number of questions evaluated beyond the counts in the table, how questions were sampled, whether the experts were blind to system identity, how disagreements were resolved, or what inter-annotator agreement was. The four-point rating scale is reasonable, but the evaluation seems to compare only the proposed method against one baseline, not against all relevant variants. It also does not include human-written answers as an upper bound. Without these details, it is difficult to interpret the reported improvement. The deployment statistic that 455 out of 1,492 answers were judged “Good” is interesting, but it is not a controlled evaluation and may be affected by user expectations, interface effects, or selection bias.


The baselines and ablations are also incomplete. The comparison includes LSTM answer selection and variants of the proposed method, but the paper does not compare against simpler retrieval or composition baselines that might be quite strong. For example, selecting the conclusion and supplement independently using BM25, TF-IDF, or embedding similarity; selecting the best original answer and extracting its first conclusion-like and supplement-like sentences; or using a learned pair wise ranker with handcrafted lexical features would help establish whether the neural architecture is necessary. The paper claims that the model avoids bad sentence combinations, but it would be useful to see a direct comparison to a two-stage system that first retrieves top relevant conclusions and then ranks supplements by sentence compatibility. The current ablations do not isolate the effect of the semantic embeddings, the joint loss, pertaining, and attention as cleanly as they could.


The description of the dataset raises concerns. The answer construction corpus contains about 5,000 question-conclusion-supplement examples, which is relatively small for training several LSTM models. The paper states that conclusions and supplements were extracted by human experts, while also suggesting that sentence extraction methods could be used. Since the model depends critically on these sentence-type labels, the annotation procedure is central to the task and should be described in much more detail. How were sentence types defined? Were multiple conclusions or supplements allowed per answer? How consistent were annotators? Were candidate sentence pools restricted to the love-advice category or drawn from all categories? Were near-duplicate questions or answers present across train and test folds? Since the data comes from a community Q&A site, duplicate or template answers could inflate performance if not carefully controlled.


The model presentation is sometimes unclear. The semantic embedding method is described as averaging category and title vectors with context word vectors, but the exact training objective, window size, negative sampling or softmax details, and inference-time use of category/title information are not fully specified. In the answer construction model, the notation for the loss and representations is hard to follow. The margin parameter k is stated to satisfy 0 < k < 1, but the experimental section sets k to 1.0. The loss includes several terms comparing combinations with one or both incorrect components, but it is not clear how negative conclusions and supplements are sampled, whether hard negatives are used, or how sensitive the results are to this choice. The attention mechanism also appears to compute abnormalized weights independently for each supplement token; the authors should clarify whether these weights are normalized across time, and if not, why this formulation is preferable.


The paper also lacks discussion of safety and appropriateness. A system that gives love advice to real users can influence emotionally vulnerable people. Even if the domain seems casual, advice may involve harassment, abuse, self-harm, coercion, age differences, or other sensitive situations. The proposed method recombines human-authored sentences, which can still produce inappropriate or harmful answers when taken out of context. The paper should discuss filtering, moderation, fallback behavior, and whether the predefined scenario is sufficient to avoid unsafe recommendations. This is especially important because the authors emphasize practical deployment.


I would like the authors to clarify several points. What exactly is the candidate pool at test time: all extracted conclusion and supplement sentences from the training set, from the entire Q&A archive, or from a held-out candidate set? Are sentences from the same original answer as the test question removed to avoid leakage? How many candidate conclusions and supplements are ranked per question, and how is computational tractability handled for all possible pairs? How are title tokens used for a new user question whose title may be absent or noisy? Does the semantic embedding method require the correct category at test time, and what happens if the category is wrong? In the human evaluation, were judges blind, how many questions were used, and what was inter-annotator agreement? Finally, can the authors provide examples of failure cases, especially cases where the conclusion is plausible but the supplement changes the intended meaning or produces socially inappropriate advice?


Overall, I view the paper as addressing an important and under explored problem, with a practical and intuitively sensible approach. The idea of constructing non-factoid answers from typed sentence units is worth investigating, and the domain-specific embedding component is a reasonable addition. However, the current paper overstates the generative nature of the system, does not sufficiently specify the dataset and annotation process, and relies on an evaluation that is not strong enough to establish answer quality. The experimental gains are encouraging, but the lack of strong non-neural and two-stage baselines, limited human evaluation details, and unclear risk controls make the contribution less convincing. My recommendation is weak reject. I would be more positive about a revised version that reframes the task more accurately, provides a rigorous human evaluation with blind judging and agreement statistics, includes stronger retrieval/composition baselines and ablations, and gives a fuller account of the data construction and deployment safeguards.

The paper proposes a memory-augmented attention architecture for video captioning. The central idea is to improve standard attention-based sequence-to-sequence models by maintaining a recurrent memory over previous attention decisions while generating a sentence. The model consists of three components: a temporal encoder for video frames using convolutional feature maps and location attention, a hierarchical attention/memory module that attends over the encoded frame sequence conditioned on the decoder and memory states, and a recurrent decoder that generates the next word. The authors evaluate the method on MSVD and Charades and report competitive results, including strong BLEU scores on MSVD without using optical flow, C3D features, or fine-tuned action features.

The main strength of the submission is that it addresses a real limitation of simple video captioning models: generating a caption often requires attending not just to a single frame or local temporal region, but to a temporally extended event while keeping track of what has already been described. The proposed architecture is a plausible and intuitive way to do this. The distinction between frame-level temporal encoding, spatial/location attention within convolutional maps, and a higher-level memory over generated words and attended visual states is sensible. In particular, using convolutional feature maps rather than only fully connected CNN features gives the model the capacity to attend spatially, and the recurrent memory module can in principle reduce repeated or inconsistent attention during decoding.

A second strength is that the model is trained end-to-end and does not depend on hand-engineered semantic representations or explicit action labels. This is desirable for a captioning system, since the ultimate goal is to learn the visual-to-language mapping directly. The paper also evaluates on two datasets with different properties. MSVD is a common benchmark with many reference captions, while Charades is more challenging and action-centric, with fewer captions per video. Including Charades is useful because it tests whether the model can handle more realistic activities and less dense language supervision. The qualitative examples are also helpful in illustrating both the successes and failure modes of the method.

However, I have several significant concerns about the technical clarity, novelty, and empirical support for the claims. The first issue is that the proposed memory mechanism is not explained with sufficient precision to make its contribution fully clear. The HAM module is described as storing previous attention and an encoded version of the input videos conditioned on previously generated words, but the equations suggest a relatively standard recurrent attention mechanism: attention scores are computed from the encoded video states, the previous decoder state, and the previous memory state, then the weighted visual feature is fed into an LSTM memory. It is not obvious in what sense all previous attention distributions are explicitly stored, since the memory appears to be a compressed recurrent hidden state rather than an addressable memory. The paper repeatedly contrasts this with “local” attention and claims that the model can consider the entire video while generating each word, but conventional soft attention over all encoder states already has this property. The added recurrent memory may still be useful, but the conceptual distinction should be sharpened.

Relatedly, the novelty relative to existing attention and reviewer-style captioning models is not fully convincing. Conditioning attention on prior decoder state and previous contextual information is common in sequence-to-sequence attention models. The paper argues that prior work does not explicitly store all previous attention, but the actual implementation does not seem to store the full sequence of past attention maps either. It instead summarizes prior decisions through an LSTM state. This may be a reasonable design, but the paper should be more careful in claiming a qualitatively different memory architecture. A stronger discussion would explain whether the benefit comes from adding another recurrent layer between attention and decoding, using convolutional feature maps with spatial attention, conditioning attention on a memory state, or some combination of these.

The experimental evidence does not adequately isolate these factors. The ablation table is useful, but the results are somewhat confusing and do not strongly support all claims. On MSVD, the full HAM+TEM model obtains the best METEOR among the ablations, but HAM without TEM has better BLEU-2, BLEU-3, BLEU-4, and similar CIDEr, while the simpler attention model has better CIDEr than the full model. Thus, the conclusion that TEM and HAM complement each other is only partially supported. If the full model is truly better, this should be shown consistently across metrics or explained in terms of metric tradeoffs. It would also be important to report variance over multiple runs, because the absolute differences are small and could plausibly be due to random initialization, frame sampling, or hyperparameter search.

There are also inconsistencies in the experimental setup that make interpretation difficult. The ablation study uses 40 frames per video, while the main MSVD comparison uses 8 frames per video. This makes it hard to understand how frame sampling affects the architecture, especially since the temporal encoder is one of the proposed contributions. If the temporal modeling component is important, one would expect a clear analysis of performance as a function of the number of sampled frames. The paper should also clarify whether hyperparameters were tuned separately for each ablation and baseline, or whether the full model received more tuning. Since the reported hidden sizes are quite large and selected by random search, fairness of comparison matters.

The state-of-the-art claim is overstated. On MSVD, the model has strong BLEU scores among methods that do not use external motion/action features, but it is not best on all metrics. Another method in the table has substantially higher METEOR, and methods using additional features outperform the proposed model on BLEU-4 and METEOR. It is fair to say that the method is competitive and achieves the best reported BLEU-4 among the listed methods without external features, but the abstract and conclusion claim more broadly that it outperforms all previously proposed methods and reaches a new state of the art. The tables do not support that broad statement. The Charades results show an improvement over the dataset baseline, but the gain is modest, and the absolute scores remain far below human performance. More analysis is needed before drawing strong conclusions about action understanding.

Another concern is that the paper does not provide enough implementation detail for reproducibility. The frame sampling strategy is not fully specified. The decoding procedure is also missing important details: whether greedy decoding or beam search is used, what beam size is selected, how unknown words are handled, and how maximum caption length is chosen. The preprocessing description says NLTK tokenization is used, but does not describe vocabulary thresholds, handling of rare words, start/end tokens, or whether captions are lowercased. The architecture section gives equations for the main modules, but some dimensions and definitions are unclear, especially in the attention update where repeated decoder and memory states are concatenated or broadcast across frames. The loss function omits the decoder parameters from the listed parameter set. These issues are not necessarily fatal, but they reduce confidence in the empirical results.

The qualitative examples reveal another limitation: the model can generate fluent but semantically incorrect captions, sometimes hallucinating common objects or actions. This is a known issue in captioning systems, but it is particularly relevant here because the paper claims that the memory-augmented attention improves reasoning over actions and temporal structure. Some of the incorrect examples suggest that the model may rely heavily on language priors. A more convincing evaluation would include attention visualizations over time and space, showing whether the model attends to action-relevant frames when generating verbs and to object-relevant regions when generating nouns. Since the core contribution is an attention/memory mechanism, visualizing or quantitatively evaluating its behavior would be very valuable.

I have several questions for the authors. In what precise sense does the model store all previous attention decisions, rather than only a recurrent summary of them? Have you compared HAM to a standard attention decoder with an additional recurrent layer of comparable parameter count, to rule out the possibility that the gains come from increased capacity? How sensitive are the results to the number of sampled frames, and why are 40 frames used in the ablation while 8 or 16 frames are used in the main experiments? Were the ablation models given the same hyperparameter search budget as the full model? What decoding method and beam size were used for the reported results? Can the authors provide attention visualizations or an analysis showing that the memory state reduces repeated attention or improves verb generation? Finally, can the claims of state-of-the-art performance be revised to reflect the mixed results across metrics and feature settings?

Overall, I find the direction promising and the architecture reasonable, but the submission currently falls short of the level of clarity and empirical rigor I would expect for acceptance. The idea of conditioning video attention on a recurrent memory of prior attended context is worthwhile, and the results are competitive. Nevertheless, the novelty over standard recurrent attention mechanisms is not sharply established, the ablations do not clearly validate the claimed contributions, and some claims are stronger than the evidence supports. My recommendation is rejection in its current form, with encouragement to resubmit after clarifying the memory mechanism, strengthening the baselines and ablations, reporting more reproducible experimental details, and tempering the performance claims.

The paper proposes a generative modeling procedure in which samples are produced by repeatedly applying a learned stochastic transition operator in data space, starting from simple factorial noise. The central idea is “infusion training”: during training, the Markov chain is not run exactly as it will be at generation time, but is slightly biased toward a chosen target training example by occasionally replacing individual coordinates with values derived from that target. The transition operator is then trained to map intermediate infused states toward the target. At test time, the infusion is removed, and the learned chain is expected to progressively denoise random noise into a realistic sample. The paper positions this method relative to denoising autoencoders, generative stochastic networks, variational autoencoders, and diffusion-like inverse processes. Experiments on MNIST, TFD, CIFAR-10, and CelebA show visually plausible samples, along with quantitative comparisons using Parzen estimates, likelihood lower bounds or importance sampling estimates, and an inpainting demonstration.

The main strength of the paper is the simplicity and appeal of the core idea. Learning a data-space Markov transition that transforms unstructured noise into structured samples is intuitive, and the infusion mechanism provides a practical way to generate informative training pairs without requiring an explicit encoder or an adversarial discriminator. The method is also conceptually distinct from slow noising and denoising processes because the training chain follows the model as it evolves rather than being tied to a fixed corruption process. This adaptive aspect is appealing: as the transition operator improves, the infused states become more model-like, which may help the learner focus on states it will actually encounter at generation time.

A second strength is that the method occupies an attractive middle ground between several families of generative models. It avoids the minimax instability of adversarial training, does not require exact tractable likelihood, and can generate samples in a relatively small number of steps compared with very slow diffusion-like procedures. The use of a single denoising network trained with a straightforward supervised objective is practically appealing. The paper also makes a reasonable effort to connect the heuristic denoising objective to a variational lower bound and to provide both a denoising-based and lower-bound-based formulation. This helps clarify that the method is not merely a sampling trick but can be understood as defining a latent-variable model with an approximate posterior-like proposal distribution.

The empirical results are promising, especially the qualitative samples. The MNIST and face samples appear sharp and varied, and the inpainting results on CelebA suggest that the learned transition operator captures meaningful conditional structure rather than simply producing unconditional prototypes. The visualization of the training chains is also helpful and provides insight into what infusion is doing. The model gradually learns to exploit small target cues and amplify them into coherent images. This is one of the most convincing parts of the paper because it illustrates the mechanism rather than only reporting final samples.

The paper’s main weakness is that the training objective remains only partially justified. The denoising-based infusion procedure trains every intermediate state to map directly to the final target example, yet at generation time the model is never given infused target information. The paper argues that the infused chain stays close to the model chain, but this closeness depends critically on the infusion schedule, dimensionality, and the model’s current behavior. Since injecting even a small probability per coordinate may reveal substantial global information in high-dimensional images, the actual training distribution may differ substantially from the sampling distribution. I would like to see a more careful analysis of this mismatch. For example, what is the expected fraction of target-derived pixels at each step, and how does that compare to the amount of structure present in a typical model-generated state? Does the network learn a robust denoising field, or does it learn to exploit artificial coordinate-level hints that are absent at test time?

The likelihood discussion is also somewhat confusing and not fully convincing. The paper derives a lower bound using the infused chain as a proposal distribution, but most experiments appear to use the heuristic denoising objective rather than directly optimizing this bound. It is therefore unclear how much the reported likelihood estimates reflect the training objective. Some likelihood numbers are unusually large and difficult to interpret, especially in comparison with other reported estimates. The distinction between the diagonal Gaussian output model and the isotropic variant is important but not fully explored. Since likelihood estimates are central to evaluating non-adversarial generative models, the paper should be more explicit about the exact observation model, variance treatment, dequantization, number of importance samples, and whether parameters such as variances are learned or fixed. Comparisons to other methods would be more persuasive if the same evaluation protocol and architecture capacity were used wherever possible.

The experimental section would also benefit from more systematic ablations. The method introduces several important design choices: the infusion rate schedule, the number of training steps, the number of sampling steps, whether parameters are shared across time, the weighting of losses at different steps, variance scaling, and the use of batch normalization with separate statistics per time step. These choices may strongly affect performance, yet the paper provides little evidence about sensitivity. For example, the authors often sample for more steps than the model was trained for, which may improve sample sharpness but raises questions about stability and about what distribution is actually being modeled. What happens if the chain is run for many more steps? Does it converge, diverge, collapse to modes, or continue sharpening? Since the method is framed as a Markov chain but is not required to have a useful stationary distribution, behavior beyond the chosen horizon is still important for understanding robustness.

The comparison to adversarial models and diffusion-like methods is suggestive but not entirely fair. The paper claims competitive results with a basic GAN, and the CIFAR inception score exceeds a particular unsupervised baseline, but the samples shown do not clearly establish competitiveness with strong convolutional generative models. The architectures used for different datasets are not discussed in enough detail to isolate the contribution of infusion training from the choice of generator network. In particular, using a transposed-convolution architecture derived from GAN work may account for some of the sample quality. Conversely, if the method is intended as a training procedure rather than a specific architecture, the paper should demonstrate that it remains effective across architectures and that improvements are due to infusion rather than capacity or tuning.

There is also a concern about memorization and diversity. Showing nearest neighbors is useful but not sufficient to establish that the model has learned a broad distribution rather than a smoothed or recombined set of training examples. This is especially relevant because the training procedure repeatedly conditions on individual target examples and injects their coordinates. More quantitative diversity measures, interpolation or chain-branching analyses, and nearest-neighbor comparisons in feature space would strengthen the empirical case. The inpainting results partially address this concern because multiple plausible completions are shown for the same top half, but a larger-scale evaluation would be preferable.

I have several specific questions for the authors. How sensitive is performance to the infusion schedule, especially the final infusion probability? In high dimensions, does a per-pixel infusion probability of a few percent provide enough target information to make the task much easier than the true generation task? During lower-bound-based training, do the authors backpropagate through samples from the infused proposal distribution, and if so, how is the dependence of the proposal on model parameters handled? How do results compare when optimizing the lower bound directly rather than using the denoising surrogate across all datasets? What happens when the chain is run for 2x, 5x, or 10x the training horizon? Does the model remain on the data manifold? Can the authors report ablations for shared versus unshared batch-normalization statistics across time? Finally, how much computation is required relative to GANs, VAEs, and slow diffusion-like models for comparable sample quality?

Overall, I find the paper creative and worth discussing. The infusion idea is novel, intuitive, and potentially useful, and the visual results indicate that the approach can learn meaningful generative transitions in relatively few steps. At the same time, the current version relies heavily on heuristic motivation and qualitative evidence, and some of the quantitative evaluation is difficult to interpret. The paper would be substantially stronger with clearer likelihood evaluation, sensitivity studies, and a more direct analysis of the mismatch between infused training chains and non-infused generation chains. My recommendation is a weak accept. The contribution is original and promising enough for presentation, but the empirical and theoretical support should be strengthened to make the method’s advantages more convincing.

The paper proposes a video prediction model that predicts local transformations rather than future pixels. The method first estimates affine transformations between adjacent frames on a dense grid of overlapping patches, then trains a convolutional network to predict future affine transform fields from past transform fields. At test time, predicted transforms are applied to the most recent frame to synthesize future frames.
