The paper argues that this avoids the blurriness typical of pixel-space regression because uncertainty is handled in transformation space, where averaging errors may still correspond to plausible motion. A second contribution is an evaluation protocol in which generated frames are passed to an action classifier trained on real videos, with classification accuracy used as a proxy for whether the generated sequence preserves discriminative content. Experiments on moving MNIST and UCF-101 suggest that the proposed method produces sharper outputs than pixel-regression or adversarial baselines and obtains higher downstream classification accuracy while using substantially fewer parameters and computation.


The main strength of the paper is the clarity and simplicity of the central idea. Modeling local motion explicitly is a natural and appealing alternative to direct pixel prediction, and the paper presents a concrete instantiation that is easy to understand. The decomposition into affine transform extraction, transform prediction, and frame reconstruction makes the approach modular. The use of overlapping patches is also sensible: it provides more flexibility than a single global transform while retaining a structured, low-dimensional prediction target. The unrolled multi-step training procedure is another positive aspect, since it directly addresses the train-test mismatch that arises when a one-step predictor is recursively applied at test time.


The paper is also valuable in emphasizing that pixel MSE is a poor measure for video prediction quality. The proposed classifier-based evaluation is a practical attempt to define a task-oriented alternative. The reported difference between ground-truth frames and reconstructions from ground-truth affine transforms is particularly useful: it suggests that for the tested frame sizes and patch parameters, the affine patch representation preserves much of the information needed by the action classifier. The comparison to a copy-last-frame baseline, optical flow extrapolation, and a stronger learned video prediction model is also helpful. The computational efficiency of the method is another significant advantage. If the reported parameter and floating-point operation counts are accurate and comparable, the approach is an attractive baseline for future work.


However, I have several concerns that make the paper less convincing. The largest issue is that the proposed evaluation protocol is not sufficient on its own to establish the quality of video prediction. Action classification accuracy on generated frames may reflect preservation of static appearance more than realistic temporal dynamics. This concern is supported by the high score of the copy-last-frame baseline, especially when using only four generated frames. Many UCF-101 classes can be recognized from scene context, objects, clothing, or pose, and the classifier may not be very sensitive to subtle motion artifacts. Thus, a model that simply keeps frames sharp and preserves the actor and background may score well even if it substantially underestimates or distorts motion. The paper acknowledges some underestimation of movement, but the proposed metric may not penalize this sufficiently.


The classifier-based metric has a potentially ambiguous interpretation. If the goal is to evaluate whether generated videos are plausible continuations, then retaining discriminative features is only one requirement. A generated sequence could preserve the class label while being dynamically implausible, temporally inconsistent, or nearly static. Conversely, a plausible future may change the appearance in a way that makes the classifier less confident, particularly for actions requiring longer temporal context. It would strengthen the paper to include additional quantitative measures targeted at temporal realism, such as flow statistics, human preference studies, or comparisons under classifiers trained to distinguish real from generated sequences. A careful breakdown by action category would help determine whether the gains arise from motion-sensitive classes or mostly from appearance-dominated ones.


A second concern is the limited experimental depth. The moving MNIST results are purely qualitative, and the UCF-101 experiments use a single main downstream classifier. There is no statistical analysis or indication of variance across splits, generated samples, or training runs. It is also unclear how sensitive the results are to the affine extraction procedure, which appears to be a crucial part of the system. The ablations on patch size, input frames, and prediction horizon are useful, but they are relatively shallow. For example, the paper reports classification accuracy changes but not reconstruction quality of the affine extraction step, transform prediction error, or the relationship between transform-space MSE and visual quality. Since the method depends heavily on the assumption that local affine transforms are a good representation, more detailed quantitative analysis of that assumption would be valuable.


The affine transform extractor itself needs more explanation. The paper describes estimating a set of affine mappings by minimizing reconstruction error, but it does not provide enough detail about the optimization procedure, initialization, convergence criteria, boundary handling, regularization, or computational cost. Are the affine transforms estimated independently for every pair of frames in the training set through iterative optimization? If so, how expensive is this preprocessing stage compared with training a direct prediction model? Are there failure cases when illumination changes, occlusions, or non-rigid deformations cause poor local affine estimates? Since the learned predictor is trained on these estimated transforms, noise or ambiguity in the extraction stage could substantially affect performance. The paper would benefit from a more complete description and analysis of this stage.


The method also has inherent representational limitations that deserve more serious discussion. Applying local transforms to the previous frame cannot synthesize newly visible content, model disocclusions, handle significant out-of-plane rotations, or represent appearance changes except through warping. Averaging overlapping transformed patches may hide some artifacts, but it cannot solve the missing-content problem. On short horizons this may be acceptable, but for longer prediction it should cause degradation. The paper reports results up to eight frames, but the conclusions sometimes sound broader than the evidence supports. It would be useful to see how quickly the method fails as the prediction horizon increases, and whether failures are qualitatively different from those of pixel-space models.


The comparison to prior learned prediction models is not fully satisfying. The paper compares to a multi-scale adversarial model and notes that it is much larger and more expensive. However, it is not entirely clear whether all models were trained under comparable conditions, with the same input preprocessing, frame resolution, number of conditioning frames, and prediction horizon. Since the proposed method predicts transformations extracted from frames, while the baseline predicts pixels, the training targets and losses differ substantially. This is the point of the paper, but it also makes fairness important. More details about baseline implementation, hyperparameter tuning, and whether the reported results are from retraining or from existing models would improve confidence.


I also find the claim that MSE in transformation space avoids averaging somewhat overstated. The paper itself observes that the model underestimates movement, which is exactly an averaging effect. Predicting the mean of plausible transformations may produce a sharp frame because the previous image is warped rather than regressed, but it can still yield an implausible or overly conservative trajectory. This is a meaningful distinction, but the paper should be more precise: the approach avoids pixel blurring due to averaging in image space, not uncertainty averaging more generally. If multiple futures are possible, a deterministic MSE-trained transform predictor still has the usual conditional mean problem. Modeling a distribution over transformations, as mentioned in the conclusion, seems important rather than merely a future extension.


I have several specific questions for the authors. How exactly are the affine transforms estimated in practice, and how long does this preprocessing take on UCF-101? Are the reported computational savings measured only for the prediction network, or do they include affine extraction for conditioning frames at test time? How sensitive is the method to the stride and overlap used in patch reconstruction? Does the classifier-based evaluation use only generated frames, or are the conditioning frames also included as input to the classifier? If conditioning frames are excluded, how are short clips aligned with the classifier’s expected temporal input? If they are included, how much of the classification accuracy comes from the real conditioning frames rather than the generated continuation? Can the authors report category-level accuracies, especially for actions where temporal motion is essential? Finally, can the authors provide quantitative results for longer horizons, even if performance degrades, to clarify the useful operating range of the method?


Overall, I find the paper interesting and potentially useful, particularly as a strong and efficient baseline for short-term video prediction. The central idea of predicting local transformations is intuitive, well motivated, and produces sharper outputs than direct pixel regression. The proposed evaluation protocol is also a worthwhile contribution in that it moves away from pixel MSE. At the same time, the empirical case is not yet fully convincing. The evaluation may reward preservation of appearance more than realistic motion, the affine extraction stage is under-specified, and the method’s limitations for occlusion, disocclusion, and long-term prediction are not sufficiently quantified. My recommendation is therefore borderline, leaning weak reject in its current form. With a more rigorous evaluation of temporal realism, clearer implementation details, and stronger baseline comparisons, this could become a compelling contribution.

The paper studies whether a convolutional network trained with weak labels on rendered tic-tac-toe boards can acquire representations corresponding to game concepts such as board locations, legal turns, and winning moves. The authors generate all legal tic-tac-toe states in which one player can win on the next move, render them as images, assign one of 18 labels corresponding to the winning side and location, train an AlexNet-style classifier with global average pooling, and visualize class activation maps (CAMs). They observe that the resulting CAMs often highlight the empty square where the winning move should be placed, even though the network was not directly trained with pixel-level localization labels. Other variants add grid lines or alter the appearance of the pieces, and the paper proposes two quantitative measures intended to capture whether the activation map corresponds to the correct move. The authors interpret the results as evidence that game concepts can emerge under weak supervision, that cross-modal supervision ideas extend to higher-level action and game-rule semantics, and that CAM can activate on non-salient image regions.

The paper has an appealing central intuition. Tic-tac-toe is simple enough that the full state space can be enumerated, yet it still contains nontrivial relational structure: lines, turns, two players, and outcomes. Using such a controlled domain to probe what a CNN learns is a reasonable and potentially useful way to study representation learning, visualization, and weak supervision without the confounds of large natural-image datasets. The observation that activation maps can peak at empty board locations is also interesting at a qualitative level. It highlights that discriminative spatial evidence in CNNs need not coincide with object texture or local saliency, but can arise from contextual information in the receptive field. The paper is clearly motivated and tries to connect the experiment to several broader questions in deep reinforcement learning, cross-modal supervision, and interpretability. The authors also deserve credit for proposing quantitative measures rather than relying only on hand-picked visualizations, and for including appearance-change controls and an intermediate-training analysis.

However, I find the main claims substantially overstated relative to the evidence provided. The most serious issue is that the supervision is not as weak as the paper suggests. The labels are 18 classes corresponding directly to the player and square of the winning next move. Although the network is not given a segmentation mask or an explicit rule table, the class label effectively encodes the answer to the localization and action problem. In this setting, a CAM that highlights the square associated with a class is not strong evidence that the network has discovered a game concept in the sense of understanding the rule. It may simply have learned discriminative visual templates associated with each of the 18 output classes. Since the task is restricted to positions where the game ends in one move, the input-output mapping can be solved by recognizing local and semi-local patterns of pieces, without representing the full rules of tic-tac-toe.

The paper's terminology around "concepts" is therefore problematic. The claim that the network learns the concept of a grid, the winning rule, and two sides requires stronger behavioral tests. For example, if a representation truly encoded the winning rule, it should transfer to states where the best move blocks an opponent, creates a fork, or avoids a trap; it should generalize to board transformations or different board sizes; it should allow accurate play beyond immediate wins; and it should be usable by a simple downstream model to infer legal and winning moves under held-out configurations. The current experiment considers only a narrow subset of positions and evaluates whether the most activated region matches the already encoded class. This is closer to weakly supervised localization of a class-correlated location than to concept learning.

A second major concern is the lack of clarity about train/test separation and generalization. The paper reports 100% classification accuracy on the tic-tac-toe dataset, but it is not clear whether this is training accuracy, validation accuracy, or accuracy on a held-out set of states. Since the dataset contains only 1029 samples for the main task and is synthetically generated from a small finite state space, memorization is a real possibility. The quantitative localization scores are also not described in enough detail to assess whether they are computed on training examples or held-out examples. If all CAM visualizations and RAC/RCO scores are measured on the same examples used for fine-tuning, they provide weak evidence about learned structure. A convincing study would need held-out board states, possibly held-out board symmetries, and controls that test whether the network is learning rules rather than associating visual templates with labels.

The use of CAM as evidence for internal understanding is also not sufficiently justified. CAM is a visualization of class-discriminative spatial contributions under a particular architecture, not a direct readout of semantic concepts. A highlighted empty square may arise because the feature at that spatial location has a receptive field covering the relevant row, column, or diagonal, not because the model represents "this is the winning move" in a conceptually meaningful way. This observation is still interesting, but it should be framed more cautiously. In addition, the paper does not compare CAM to other attribution methods, occlusion tests, gradient-based saliency, or direct probing of intermediate features. Without such controls, it is difficult to know whether the heatmaps reflect robust model reasoning or artifacts of the visualization method, the rendering geometry, the receptive field layout, padding, or class-specific templates.

The experimental design would benefit from several additional baselines. A linear classifier or multilayer perceptron on symbolic 3-by-3 board encodings could establish the intrinsic difficulty of the classification task. A CNN trained from scratch, rather than fine-tuned from ImageNet AlexNet, would test whether pretraining is relevant in this synthetic domain. A model trained with randomly permuted labels, or with labels that encode only the side but not the square, would help determine whether CAM localization is genuinely tied to the game structure. A baseline that chooses the square from simple pattern matching or from a non-convolutional classifier would contextualize the RAC values. Since the board states are tiny and discrete, the paper should also discuss whether the CNN is exploiting absolute spatial position and fixed rendering conventions rather than learning a reusable rule.

The quantitative evaluation is not yet convincing. Representation accuracy is intuitive, but the definition of "most activated patch" depends on map resolution, interpolation, patch size, and tie-breaking, and these details are missing. Representation consistency is less clear: the reported values are negative and scaled by 10^3, but their interpretation is not explained. If higher consistency means better alignment with an ideal activation map, the table should make that explicit and should report confidence intervals, or at least variation across random initializations. The improvements from adding grid lines are plausible, but the paper attributes them to the network being given an implied board concept. An alternative explanation is simply that grid lines provide strong spatial anchors and high-frequency gradients at fixed locations, making localization by CAM easier. That is not necessarily evidence of a more semantic representation.

The connection to cross-modal supervision is also weak. In the described experiments, the labels are generated by a simulator from the same underlying board state that is rendered as an image. This is not really a separate modality in the sense used by the cited cross-modal learning papers; it is a symbolic target derived from the game engine. The paper may still be about weakly supervised representation learning from automatically generated labels, but the cross-modal framing does not add much and may obscure the contribution. Similarly, the analogy to deep reinforcement learning is only motivational. The experiments do not involve reinforcement learning, sequential decision-making, rewards, exploration, or policies. The claim that this helps answer whether a DRL network "understands" game concepts is therefore indirect at best.

I would like the authors to clarify several points. Are the reported classification and localization results computed on held-out states, or on the training set? How are train/validation/test splits constructed, and are symmetric rotations and reflections of a board allowed to appear across splits? What exactly is the label generation procedure for states with multiple winning moves, and how many such cases exist? Does the network receive only positions where the next move wins, or does it also see non-winning states during training? How is the ideal activation map defined for RCO, and why are the reported correlations negative? How sensitive are RAC and RCO to random initialization, crop and rendering details, CAM resolution, and pretraining? Finally, can the learned representation be used to select moves in states that do not end the game immediately, or to transfer to a different rendering without retraining?

Overall, I appreciate the simplicity and creativity of the experimental setup, and I think the observation about CAM activation on empty squares is worth reporting in a more focused paper on visualization behavior in synthetic relational domains. However, the current version makes much stronger claims about concept emergence, rule understanding, and cross-modal higher-level semantics than the experiments support. The task labels already encode the desired action, the evaluation appears limited to a small and possibly non-held-out dataset, and the CAM evidence is not sufficient to infer conceptual understanding. My recommendation is rejection in its present form. A revised version with rigorous held-out generalization tests, stronger baselines, clearer metrics, causal attribution experiments, and more cautious claims could become a useful case study in weakly supervised localization and the probing of CNN representations.

The paper proposes an iterative refinement approach for machine translation in which an existing complete translation is repeatedly edited by substituting individual words. The motivation is that standard left-to-right or bottom-up decoders commit to earlier decisions and cannot directly revisit them, whereas a post-hoc refinement model can condition on the entire current hypothesis and the source sentence. The authors first study a word-level error detection model, then introduce a convolutional attention-based model that predicts a replacement word at each target position given the source and surrounding target context, they then finally extend this to a dual-attention model that additionally attends to the initial guess translation. At test time, the model proposes substitutions for all positions, a heuristic or classifier scores the proposed edits, and the highest-scoring edit is applied until a threshold or maximum number of edits is reached. On WMT15 German-English, starting from a phrase-based MT system, the best method improves BLEU by about 0.3–0.4 across several test sets while changing fewer than one word per sentence on average.


The main appeal of the paper is the clear formulation of translation refinement as a distinct decoding paradigm. Rather than treating translation as a strictly generative left-to-right process, the authors explore a model that has access to a full candidate output and can revise local decisions. This is a natural and potentially important direction. It addresses a real limitation of conventional decoding: many translation errors become apparent only after later context is generated, and a model that can see both left and right context may be better suited to correcting agreement, word choice, punctuation, or local semantic errors. The paper is also admirably concrete. The proposed models are simple enough to understand, the refinement procedure is explicit, and the experiments include several useful variants, such as single-source attention, dual attention, alternative scoring heuristics, and oracle analyses.


Another strength is that the paper does not rely on manually post-edited data. The models are trained from parallel data and system-generated hypotheses, which makes the approach more scalable than conventional automatic post-editing setups requiring human corrections of machine output. The training procedure for generating initial guesses on the training set is also thoughtfully designed: using multiple phrase-based systems trained on disjoint subsets helps avoid the unrealistic situation in which the baseline system translates sentences it has already seen during training. This is an important experimental detail and strengthens the credibility of the reported improvements.


The dual-attention model is a sensible architectural extension. Conditioning not only on the source and reference-side context during training, but also on the system’s current guess, reduces the discrepancy between training and testing and gives the model a mechanism for copying words that are already correct. The reported perplexity reduction from the single-attention model to the dual-attention model is convincing evidence that the guess translation provides useful information. The empirical finding that the dual model is more conservative also fits the task: when starting from a strong baseline, most words should be left unchanged, and a refinement model must avoid unnecessary edits. The qualitative examples further illustrate that the model can correct meaningful lexical errors, punctuation, tense, and pronoun choices.


The oracle experiments are also valuable. They show that the model’s proposed substitutions contain more potential improvement than the deployed heuristic extracts, and that the decision of when and where to edit is a major bottleneck. This analysis helps separate two issues: whether the word prediction model can suggest useful alternatives, and whether the refinement strategy can reliably choose beneficial substitutions. The gap between the heuristic method and the oracle suggests that the general idea has room for improvement, rather than merely reaching a hard limit.


However, the paper’s empirical contribution is quite modest in its current form. The best reported improvement is only around 0.4 BLEU, with mean gains of about 0.32 BLEU over the WMT test sets. While the consistency across test sets is encouraging, the magnitude is small, and the paper does not report statistical significance testing. Given the known variance of BLEU under small translation changes, it is difficult to judge whether the gains are robust enough to justify the added modelling and decoding complexity. The method modifies only about 0.6 words per sentence, which is interesting, but it also means the overall effect is limited. The paper would be much stronger if it included significance tests, bootstrap confidence intervals, or a more detailed breakdown of the types of edits that contribute to the BLEU gains.


The restriction to word substitutions is a serious limitation. Many translation errors require insertions, deletions, phrase-level replacements, reordering, or changes in segmentation. Several of the examples demonstrate exactly this problem: the model sometimes replaces a word because it cannot insert a missing function word or restructure the phrase, leading to unnatural or only partially improved output. Since the approach is motivated by human-like revision, limiting the operation set to one-for-one substitutions makes the refinement process much less expressive than the motivation suggests. The authors acknowledge this in future work, but the limitation directly affects the central claims. It is not clear whether a substitution-only model can scale beyond minor lexical clean-up.


The comparison setting is also narrow. The method is evaluated only on German-to-English and only starting from a phrase-based translation system. In 2017, neural machine translation systems are already strong baselines, and the paper’s motivation partly concerns neural left-to-right decoding. It would therefore be important to evaluate refinement on top of a neural baseline, or at least to discuss why phrase-based output is the main test case. Phrase-based systems have different error profiles from neural systems, and a model trained to fix PBMT output may mostly learn to repair local lexical and fluency errors. It remains unclear whether the approach would help strong attention-based neural systems, where errors may be more global, more fluent but semantically wrong, or related to omissions. The paper would also benefit from experiments using weaker initial guesses, stronger initial guesses, or multiple guess translations, to clarify the conditions under which iterative refinement is useful.


The novelty relative to automatic post-editing and confidence estimation is somewhat underdeveloped. The authors distinguish their approach by not requiring post-edited data, but the method still resembles monolingual or bilingual post-editing of MT output with a neural model. The paper could do more to position itself against prior work on system combination, rescoring, lattice or n-best list refinement, and post-editing trained from synthetic data. In particular, if the baseline system can produce n-best lists or lattices, a strong rescoring or minimum-risk decoding baseline might capture some of the same corrections without requiring a separate iterative editing procedure. The paper does not compare against such alternatives, making it hard to assess whether the proposed decoding scheme is the best way to use this information.


The refinement selection mechanism is another weak point. The authors test simple confidence heuristics and a small classifier, but the classifier appears to use only two log-probability features. This seems underpowered for deciding whether an edit improves translation quality. Important features such as position, source attention entropy, local language model scores, lexical identity, edit type, baseline confidence, and sentence-level context are ignored. Since the oracle analysis shows that edit selection is a major bottleneck, the paper should either present a stronger learned selection model or more clearly frame the current results as preliminary. It is also unclear how thresholds and maximum edit counts generalize across domains, language pairs, and baseline systems.


The evaluation relies almost entirely on BLEU. Because the system makes very few substitutions, BLEU may reward edits that move the output closer to the single reference even if they do not improve adequacy or fluency, and may fail to reward legitimate improvements not reflected in the reference. The error detection section itself notes the problem of single-reference evaluation, but the final evaluation does not address it. A human evaluation or targeted analysis of accepted and rejected edits would be helpful. For instance, what fraction of edits are genuinely adequacy-improving, fluency-improving, neutral paraphrases, or harmful? Are the improvements concentrated in punctuation and morphology, or do they include substantive lexical corrections? Do repeated edits ever cause oscillations or degradation? These details would make the contribution more convincing.


I have several specific questions for the authors. How sensitive are the results to the strength and type of the initial translation system? Would the same refinement model improve a neural MT baseline, or does it mainly correct artefacts of phrase-based translation? Were the BLEU improvements tested for statistical significance on each test set and on the concatenation of all test sets? How often does the model propose the identity substitution, and how often are proposed substitutions rejected by the threshold? What is the runtime cost of iterative refinement relative to baseline decoding? Does the model ever cycle between alternatives across iterations, and if so how is that handled? Why does the learned classifier not outperform the simple product heuristic, and were richer features tried? Finally, can the authors provide an edit-type analysis showing which categories of substitutions account for most of the BLEU improvement?


Overall, I find the idea promising and the paper well written, but I am not fully convinced by the empirical evidence. The work presents a clean and interesting formulation of iterative translation refinement, and the consistency of the small gains suggests that the method is doing something meaningful. At the same time, the improvements are very small, the operation set is limited, the baselines are narrow, and the evaluation does not yet establish that the method is broadly useful beyond minor corrections to phrase-based output. My recommendation is a weak reject in its current form. I would be more positive with stronger comparisons, significance testing, evaluation on neural baselines, and a more capable edit selection model or broader edit space.

The paper proposes a reinforcement learning framework for solving combinatorial optimization problems with neural networks, focusing primarily on the Euclidean traveling salesman problem and briefly demonstrating applicability to Knapsack. The central idea is to use a pointer-network-style recurrent model as a stochastic policy over permutations, train it with policy gradients using tour length as the reward signal, and then improve inference by either sampling many candidate tours or performing “active search,” in which the policy parameters are further optimized on a single test instance. The paper argues that this avoids the need for supervised optimal labels, can learn useful heuristics from reward alone, and provides a relatively general recipe for neural approaches to combinatorial search. Empirically, the method substantially improves over a supervised pointer network baseline and obtains near-optimal results on random 2D Euclidean TSP instances up to 100 nodes when allowed extensive sampling or test-time optimization.


This is an interesting and timely paper. Its main contribution is not that it competes with specialized TSP solvers, which it explicitly does not, but that it demonstrates a plausible way to train neural combinatorial policies directly against an optimization objective rather than by imitation of an external solver. This distinction is important. Supervised learning from solver outputs can be brittle, expensive and misaligned with the ultimate objective, since reproducing a particular tour is not the same as finding any short tour. Framing the problem as reinforcement learning is natural, and the paper’s results show that this choice matters: the RL-trained policies outperform the supervised pointer-network approach by a large margin. The active search component is also conceptually appealing, since combinatorial optimization at test time is inherently a search problem, and the paper shows that gradient-based adaptation of the neural policy can be used as a form of instance-specific search.


A second strength is that the empirical presentation is relatively transparent about the limitations of the method. The authors compare against exact and heuristic TSP solvers and clearly state that their method is much slower than Concorde or strong local search methods. This honesty makes the paper more credible. The comparisons to Christofides-style heuristics, generic OR tools, and optimal solutions provide useful context for assessing where the learned method stands. The percentile plots of tour length ratios are particularly helpful, since average tour length alone can hide whether a method is consistently near-optimal or only occasionally succeeds. The paper also examines several inference configurations, including greedy decoding, ensembling, sampling, active search from a pretrained policy, and active search from scratch. This helps disentangle the contribution of learned initialization from the contribution of sheer test-time computation.


The paper is also significant because it opens a broader research direction. The proposed architecture and training recipe suggest that neural networks can learn reusable construction heuristics for structured discrete problems, and the use of a pointer mechanism is a reasonable architectural choice for problems whose solutions are permutations or subsets of inputs. Even though the Knapsack experiment appears less developed than the TSP section, it supports the claim that the approach is not entirely specific to tours. The discussion of feasibility constraints and penalties is useful, and it shows that the authors are aware of the challenges in extending the method to more constrained combinatorial settings.


That said, the paper has several important weaknesses. The most serious is that the empirical evidence for general combinatorial optimization is still narrow. The main experiments are on random Euclidean TSP instances with points sampled uniformly in the unit square. This distribution is standard and convenient, but it is also much simpler than many real routing problems and may strongly favor learning geometric regularities. It is unclear how well the learned policy transfers to non-uniform point distributions, clustered instances, non-Euclidean metrics, asymmetric TSP, or instances with additional constraints. Since the paper’s motivation emphasizes generality and reduced heuristic engineering, the evaluation should stress-test distribution shift more directly. A policy trained on uniform random TSP instances might be learning a highly distribution-specific geometric prior rather than a broadly useful optimization strategy.


The second major weakness is computational efficiency. The best results rely on sampling or active search over very large numbers of candidate solutions, with reported runtimes that are orders of magnitude worse than specialized solvers and often worse than reasonably generic local search. This does not invalidate the research contribution, but it weakens some of the practical framing. In particular, if a method requires 1.28 million sampled tours or many thousands of gradient updates per test instance, it is difficult to view it as an effective solver rather than as an interesting proof of concept. The paper would benefit from a more careful analysis of the tradeoff between computation and solution quality, especially relative to simple baselines that also scale with test-time budget, such as randomized 2-opt, repeated nearest-neighbor starts, or local improvement applied to neural samples. The comparison to OR tools is useful, but the neural method’s advantage over some generic baselines may disappear under equal wall-clock or equal candidate-evaluation budgets.


The active search method is intriguing but not fully understood. Optimizing the policy on a single instance from sampled rewards resembles a learned stochastic local search procedure, but the paper does not provide enough analysis of why it works, when it fails, or what role the pretrained policy plays beyond initializing a distribution biased toward good tours. For TSP50, sampling appears competitive with or better than active search, while for TSP100 active search helps more. This difference deserves explanation. Does active search mainly improve because it reduces entropy around good regions, because it learns instance-specific geometric structure, or because it acts like an adaptive sampling temperature? How sensitive is it to learning rate, baseline decay, batch size, number of glimpses, and input shuffling? Since active search is one of the paper’s distinctive ideas, stronger ablation and diagnostic experiments would make the contribution much clearer.


The supervised baseline also raises concerns. The authors state that their supervised implementation trained on optimal tours performs worse than the results reported in prior work and that learning from optimal tours may be harder due to subtle features. This is plausible, but not entirely satisfying. Since one of the main empirical claims is that RL substantially improves over supervised learning, the supervised baseline needs to be as strong and carefully tuned as possible. It would be useful to compare supervised pretraining followed by RL fine-tuning, imitation of multiple optimal or near-optimal tours when symmetries exist, and supervised training with the same search procedures used for the RL policy. Without these controls, it is hard to know whether the improvement comes from reinforcement learning or from the use of test-time sampling and optimization.


The paper’s claims about generality should also be moderated. The pointer network architecture is well matched to selecting elements of an input sequence, especially when feasibility can be enforced by masking previously selected items. However, many combinatorial optimization problems have richer state-dependent constraints, graph structures, or solution representations that are not naturally captured by a sequence-to-sequence model over input indices. The discussion of penalties for infeasible solutions is reasonable but speculative; for problems where feasible solutions are rare, naive penalty methods may produce extremely sparse or uninformative rewards. The Knapsack result helps, but Knapsack is still a relatively simple setting compared with vehicle routing with time windows, graph coloring, scheduling, or integer programming. Stronger evidence on a constrained problem where feasibility is genuinely difficult would better support the broad framing.


I would like the authors to clarify several points. First, how robust are the trained TSP policies to changes in the test distribution, such as clustered cities, points sampled from non-square domains, mixtures of distributions, or larger graph sizes than those used in training? Second, how much of the improvement from active search depends on random shuffling of the input sequence, and does the model’s performance vary significantly with different input orderings at greedy decoding time? Third, when using 1.28 million samples, how many distinct tours are actually produced, and how concentrated is the policy distribution before and after active search? Fourth, can the authors provide an equal-time comparison against simple randomized local-search baselines, including local improvement initialized from the model’s own tours? Such a hybrid baseline would be particularly informative, because it might reveal whether the neural network is most useful as a generator of good initial solutions rather than as a complete solver. Fifth, for Knapsack, what are the instance distributions, capacities, item value/weight distributions, and comparisons to dynamic programming or greedy baselines? The claim of optimality up to 200 items is encouraging but difficult to interpret without more details.


I would also encourage the authors to expand the ablation study. The critic architecture, number of glimpses, logit clipping, temperature tuning, model size, and batch size all appear potentially important. Since policy-gradient methods can be unstable and sensitive to variance reduction, it would be helpful to know which components are essential. For example, how much worse is a moving-average baseline than the learned critic during pretraining? Does increasing the number of glimpses improve TSP100 but hurt speed? Does the model learn meaningful geometric behavior, such as avoiding crossings, and if so at what stage of training does this emerge? Visualizing failure cases could also improve the paper, particularly for greedy decoding versus sampling and active search.


Overall, my conclusion is a weak accept. The method is not competitive with mature combinatorial optimization solvers in speed, scale, or final solution quality, and the empirical evaluation is limited mainly to uniform random Euclidean TSP. Nevertheless, the paper presents a clear and promising formulation of neural combinatorial optimization as reinforcement learning, demonstrates a substantial improvement over supervised pointer-network training, and introduces test-time active search as an interesting bridge between learned policies and instance-specific optimization. The work is likely to stimulate further research on learned heuristics, neural construction policies, and hybrid neural-search methods. I would be more enthusiastic if the paper included stronger distribution-shift experiments, more rigorous equal-compute baselines, and a deeper analysis of active search, but even in its current form it makes a worthwhile conceptual and empirical contribution.

The paper proposes compositional kernel machines ("CKMs"), an instance-based visual classification method intended to combine some advantages of kernel methods, nearest-neighbor methods, and convolutional architectures. The central idea is to compare a query image not only to observed training images, but to an exponentially large implicit set of “virtual instances” formed by recombining local elements extracted from training examples. A sum-product-function representation is used to make inference over this exponentially large set manageable under decomposability constraints. The authors define CKMs recursively in terms of leaf kernels, products over disjoint scopes, and weighted sums over common scopes, and they describe both a uniform-weight version and a discriminatively trained version using leave-one-out squared-hinge loss. Experiments on small NORB and two derived NORB variants suggest that the approach can improve over standard instance-based methods and can be competitive with some neural baselines in low-data compositional and symmetry-heavy settings.


The paper’s main strength is that it explores an interesting middle ground between classical nonparametric/kernel methods and deep compositional models. The motivation is compelling: if visual categories are compositional and exhibit local symmetries, then a method that can recombine observed parts should require fewer full-image examples than a purely global nearest-neighbor or SVM approach. The use of sum-product structure to compactly represent many possible recombinations is natural and potentially powerful. I appreciated the attempt to make the connection to sample complexity concrete: rather than merely arguing that compositions should help, the authors implement a model that can actually sum or maximize over many latent part assignments and test it on settings designed to isolate composition and symmetry.


Another positive aspect is the paper’s high-level conceptual clarity. The definition of CKMs as recursively composed sums and products is simple, and the analogy to sum-product networks and image grammars is useful. The claim that SVMs and nearest-neighbor-like methods can be viewed as special cases helps position the method. The leave-one-out training objective is also sensible for an instance-based learner, and the distinction between CKM with uniform weights and CKMW with learned weights is helpful experimentally. The paper is ambitious in trying to provide a new family of models instead of a single ad hoc architecture.


The experimental section contains some encouraging evidence. The improvement over raw nearest-neighbor methods on small NORB supports the claim that local composition can mitigate the curse of dimensionality relative to global image matching. The synthetic NORB Compositions experiment is a reasonable attempt to test whether a method can generalize from observed parts to unseen combinations. In that setting, the reported advantage over a conventionally trained convolutional network is plausible and interesting, especially given the small number of training examples. The NORB Symmetries learning curves also tell a coherent story: the proposed method appears to benefit from additional examples without requiring extensive retraining, and the learned-weight variant starts to improve as more data become available.


That said, I have several significant concerns that prevent me from being convinced by the paper in its current form. The first is that the term “kernel machine” is used somewhat loosely. The theoretical presentation begins from kernel methods and invokes kernel composition, but the actual experimental model uses a max-sum semiring and arbitrary local scoring functions, including costs and thresholds. It is not clear that the resulting discriminant corresponds to a positive semidefinite kernel, or even that the method should be analyzed as a kernel method, rather than as a latent structured matching model. This matters because some of the paper’s motivation relies on the training advantages of kernel machines, but the optimization objective is later acknowledged to be generally nonconvex. If CKMs are primarily max-product latent-composition models, then the relationship to SVMs is more limited than the framing suggests.


A related issue is that the theoretical contribution seems underdeveloped. The sum-product theorem establishes that a decomposable sum-product function can be evaluated in time linear in its size, but this is a property inherited from the chosen representation rather than a new algorithmic result specific to CKMs. The important question is how large the representation must be to capture useful visual compositions. The experimental architecture includes unary and pairwise max nodes over ORB features, but equation-level descriptions suggest potentially very large pairwise maximizations over all training elements. The paper mentions approximate nearest-neighbor structures and pruning, yet the exact computational complexity of the implemented model is not sufficiently transparent. The claim of summing over exponentially many virtual instances is formally true for decomposable structures, but it risks overstating the practical modeling power if the architecture only captures limited local pairwise structure.


The experimental evidence is also too narrow and, in places, not fully fair to the baselines. All experiments are based on NORB or constructed variants of NORB. NORB is useful for controlled studies, but it is small and stylized, and the paper makes broad claims about object recognition and alternatives to convolutional networks. The convolutional baselines are not described in enough detail to assess their strength. For example, on the NORB Compositions task the convnet achieves near-chance accuracy, but this may reflect an unsuitable architecture, insufficient augmentation, or a poor training protocol, rather than a fundamental limitation. Similarly, comparison to an SVM using features from a pretrained Inception model is awkward because those features come from a very different image distribution and may be poorly matched to grayscale toy images. The small NORB comparison uses numbers from prior work in some cases and the authors’ own implementation in others, which makes it difficult to disentangle algorithmic improvements from differences in preprocessing, feature extraction, and hyperparameter tuning.


The synthetic datasets raise further questions. The NORB Compositions task is interesting, but it is constructed in a way that may strongly favor local feature matching. If the label is determined by the identity of local toy instances appearing in an image, and if ORB descriptors can match those instances directly, then the task may be closer to multi-object instance retrieval than to semantic compositional generalization. That is still a valid problem, but it is narrower than the discussion suggests. It would be useful to know how the method performs when the relevant parts vary more substantially, when distractors are present, or when local descriptors are less reliable. The NORB Symmetries experiment similarly seems well suited to an ORB-based method because ORB descriptors already provide some rotation invariance. The paper should isolate how much of the gain comes from the CKM composition mechanism versus the hand-engineered invariances in the local features.


I also found the learning procedure insufficiently specified and evaluated. The leave-one-out objective is appealing, but it is not clear how expensive exact training is as the number of images and elements grows, especially with pairwise terms. The authors state that gradients can be computed efficiently and that small leaf-kernel values may be excluded, but the distinction between exact and approximate training is not clear in the experiments. There is little discussion of regularization, constraints on weights, initialization beyond uniform weights, or sensitivity to hyperparameters. Since the method includes ORB extraction parameters, leaf-kernel parameters, cost-function parameters, approximate search parameters, and architectural parameters, a more systematic analysis is needed. The paper criticizes convolutional networks for difficult hyperparameter tuning, but CKMs appear to introduce a substantial design space of their own.


Another concern is representational scalability. The paper argues that CKMs have advantages when new data arrive because training can be fast and instance-based. However, the model size grows with the training set, and test-time computation appears substantially slower than the reported convolutional network testing time. This tradeoff may be acceptable in some regimes, but the paper should be more precise about memory requirements, indexing costs, update costs, and test-time latency as datasets grow beyond NORB scale. In particular, the claim that adding instances and categories is easy should be supported by incremental experiments, not just asserted. For real object recognition tasks, storing and searching over all local features from all training images could become a serious bottleneck.


I would like the authors to clarify several points. In what precise sense is the experimental CKM a kernel machine? Does the max-sum version define a positive semidefinite kernel or is it better viewed as a latent matching discriminant? How much of the empirical improvement comes from ORB features alone, from local position-aware matching, from the pairwise source-image coherence cost, and from the sum-product architecture? Can the authors include ablations removing each of these components? How are hyperparameters selected, and are validation images disjoint in object identity and transformation from the test images? For the pairwise max nodes, is the maximization over all pairs of training elements actually performed, approximated by nearest-neighbor search, or pruned by thresholds? What are the memory and runtime costs as a function of the number of training images and extracted elements? Finally, can the method be evaluated on at least one less controlled dataset, or on a stronger modern convnet baseline trained with a protocol appropriate for small data?


Overall, I find the paper creative and potentially promising, but not yet convincing as a strong ICLR contribution. The core idea of implicit compositional virtual instances is interesting, and the connection to tractable sum-product evaluation could lead to useful models. However, the current submission overstates the kernel-method interpretation, relies on limited and somewhat favorable experiments, and does not provide enough ablation or scalability analysis to establish the practical value of the approach. My recommendation is "weak reject" for the main conference. I would be enthusiastic about seeing a revised version with clearer theoretical positioning, stronger baselines, more detailed computational analysis, and broader empirical validation.

The paper proposes to use a smaller “hypernetwork” to dynamically generate parameters for a larger recurrent network, focusing primarily on LSTM language modeling and sequence generation. In the recurrent setting, the hypernetwork is itself an RNN that observes the current input and previous hidden state of the main RNN, maintains its own hidden state, and outputs low-dimensional embeddings that are transformed into time-varying scaling vectors and biases for the main recurrent computation. Because fully generating dense recurrent weight matrices at every time step would be prohibitively expensive, the practical model generates row-wise scaling factors for existing weight matrices rather than arbitrary new matrices. The authors evaluate this HyperLSTM architecture on character-level Penn Treebank, enwik8, IAM handwriting generation, and WMT English-to-French translation. Across these tasks, the method improves over strong LSTM baselines and combines well with layer normalization, with particularly impressive reported results on enwik8 and machine translation.


The main strength of the paper is that it presents a compelling and scalable example of an old but underexplored idea: allowing a network to dynamically modulate the weights of another network. The formulation is simple enough to be implemented in modern sequence models, yet expressive enough to produce meaningful improvements over conventional LSTMs. I appreciate the practical compromise of generating multiplicative row-wise scaling factors rather than full matrices. This design choice makes the method computationally plausible for large RNNs and also provides a clear connection to normalization, gating, and multiplicative interaction mechanisms. The paper is not merely proposing a conceptual framework; it demonstrates a concrete version that can be trained end-to-end.


The experimental breadth is another major positive aspect. Many papers of this type would stop at a small character modeling benchmark, but this work evaluates the idea on multiple distinct sequence modeling regimes: discrete character prediction, real-valued handwriting generation, and large-scale neural machine translation. The WMT experiment is especially valuable because it shows that the approach is not restricted to toy or medium-scale settings and can improve a production-scale sequence-to-sequence architecture. The reported gain over a strong GNMT-style baseline is substantial for a single architectural modification, and the result suggests that adaptive recurrent weights can be useful even in deep, heavily engineered systems.


The discussion clarifies that HyperLSTM is not simply a second-order RNN, since the modulation is produced by a recurrent nonlinear model with its own hidden state rather than by a single linear interaction. The comparison with layer-normalized LSTMs is useful, as layer normalization is a natural alternative explanation for some of the gains. The observation that HyperLSTM and layer normalization are complementary is interesting and suggests that the hypernetwork is doing more than just rescaling activations in a static or normalization-like manner. The visualizations of the changing scaling vectors during text and handwriting generation are also helpful, but qualitative; they provide intuition that the hypernetwork learns regime changes around boundaries and uncertain regions.


There are several weaknesses that should be addressed. The largest concern is that the empirical comparisons do not always fully disentangle architectural benefit from parameter count, tuning effort, and computational cost. HyperLSTM models have more parameters than the LSTM baselines. The authors do include some larger LSTM comparisons, but the matching is not always systematic. A fairer evaluation would include baselines matched for hidden size, total parameter count, training time, and inference cost. In particular, the row-wise modulation mechanism introduces extra recurrent computation at every time step, and it would be important to know whether an ordinary LSTM with the same wall-clock budget, parameter count, or number of layers would close the gap.


The paper would also benefit from more detailed ablations. The HyperLSTM cell has several design choices: size of the hypernetwork, dimensionality of the embedding used to generate scaling vectors, whether input-to-hidden and hidden-to-hidden matrices are both modulated, whether biases are dynamically generated, and whether the hypernetwork state is necessary in addition to conditioning on the main hidden state. It is difficult to tell which of these components is responsible for the gains. For example, a strong baseline would be a non-recurrent network that produces the same scaling vectors directly from the current input and previous main hidden state. Another useful baseline would be a learned multiplicative gate applied to the preactivations of the LSTM, with a comparable number of parameters but without the interpretation of weight generation. Since the practical implementation does not generate arbitrary weights but instead produces dynamic scaling vectors, the distinction between “hypernetwork-generated weights” and “additional recurrent gates” deserves more careful empirical examination.


The novelty is promising but somewhat overstated in places. The paper frames the model as challenging weight sharing across recurrent time steps, but in the implemented version the base weight matrices are still shared and only their rows are dynamically rescaled. This is a much weaker form of non-shared weights than the introduction might suggest. It may be more precise to describe the model as learning context-dependent modulation of shared recurrent matrices. That does not diminish the usefulness of the method, but the conceptual claim should be calibrated. The connections to fast weights and multiplicative recurrent models are acknowledged, but the paper could more sharply explain what is gained over those approaches in expressivity and efficiency. In particular, the mechanism resembles a learned dynamic diagonal modulation of recurrent transformations, and the paper should clarify when this is preferable to existing multiplicative integration or input-conditioned gating methods.


The presentation of the model is mostly clear, but some details are hard to follow. The transition from the full hypernetwork formulation to the memory-efficient row-scaling version is central, yet the LSTM-specific equations are not spelled out in detail. Since most experiments use HyperLSTM rather than the simple HyperRNN, it would be helpful to provide explicit equations for all LSTM gates, including precisely where the generated scaling vectors are applied and how layer normalization is inserted when both techniques are combined. The notation around the hypernetwork inputs and embeddings is also occasionally confusing. For reproducibility, the authors should specify how the hypernetwork hidden state is initialized, whether it is reset at sequence boundaries, whether dropout is applied inside the hypernetwork, and whether the same hypernetwork generates all gate modulations or separate projections are used for each gate and matrix.


The experimental results are strong, but some comparisons need additional caution. On Penn Treebank and enwik8, the paper compares against a mixture of published results with different training procedures, regularization schemes, and evaluation protocols. The authors appropriately avoid dynamic evaluation comparisons in some cases, but the overall tables could more clearly separate directly comparable in-house baselines from external numbers. The handwriting experiment reports a very large validation log-likelihood improvement for HyperLSTM without layer normalization, but the reasons are unclear. Since layer normalization helps substantially in the language modeling experiments, why does it appear to hurt or underperform in handwriting relative to plain HyperLSTM? This might be due to interactions with mixture density outputs, sequence length, or regularization, but it deserves discussion. The machine translation result is exciting, but more details are needed on variance across runs, decoding settings, and whether the gain is robust to final training schedules. A single BLEU number for such a large system can be difficult to interpret without information about run-to-run variability.


I have several specific questions for the authors. How much of the improvement remains when comparing against LSTMs matched for total parameter count and training compute rather than hidden size? What is the inference-time overhead of HyperLSTM relative to the baseline LSTM on each task, especially in the translation experiment? Are both input-to-hidden and hidden-to-hidden matrices modulated in all experiments, and what happens if only one of them is modulated? How sensitive are the results to the hypernetwork hidden size and embedding dimension? Is the recurrent state of the hypernetwork essential, or would a feedforward conditioner using the same inputs achieve similar results? In the layer-normalized HyperLSTM, is normalization applied before or after the generated scaling factors, and is the layer normalization gain itself dynamically modulated? For machine translation, are HyperLSTM cells used in every encoder and decoder layer, including the bidirectional first encoder layer, or only in selected layers? Finally, can the authors provide any quantitative analysis of the learned scaling vectors, such as their variance, correlation across gates, or relation to token boundaries, rather than only visual examples?


Overall, I find the paper interesting, well motivated, and empirically strong. The central idea is not entirely new in spirit, and the implemented mechanism is closer to dynamic gating or row-wise modulation than to full weight generation, but the paper makes a significant contribution by showing that this family of methods can be made practical and effective at scale. The breadth of experiments and the strong results on both character modeling and machine translation make the work a valuable addition to the literature on adaptive recurrent architectures. I recommend acceptance, while encouraging the authors to temper some of the claims about non-shared weights and to add stronger ablations and compute-matched comparisons in a revision.

The paper studies the expressive power of deep neural networks through the behavior of random networks. It then uses the resulting picture to motivate several observations about trained networks. The central object is the length of a one-dimensional input trajectory after it is propagated through successive layers. The authors argue, both theoretically and empirically, that this trajectory length grows exponentially with depth for random hard-tanh networks in appropriate parameter regimes. They then connect this growth to three proposed measures of expressivity: the number of neuron transitions along an input curve, the number of activation patterns, and the number of dichotomies realized when sweeping weights along a trajectory. They also believe that earlier layers have more influence on expressivity because they have more remaining depth above them. This is tested by freezing all but one layer in networks trained on MNIST and CIFAR-10. Finally, the authors examine how training changes trajectory length, suggesting that training may trade off expressivity and stability depending on initialization scale.


The paper is ambitious and addresses an important conceptual issue: why depth appears to provide representational advantages beyond merely increasing parameter count. I found the trajectory-length perspective appealing. It gives an intuitive geometric mechanism by which depth can amplify complexity. A simple input curve can be stretched and folded repeatedly through the network, creating more crossings of decision boundaries and more activation changes. This is a useful complement to existing work based on worst-case constructions or counting linear regions. In particular, focusing on random networks is a valuable choice, since random initialization is the starting point for modern training procedures. It may also be more representative than hand-designed weights used in some expressivity separations.


A major strength of the paper is that it tries to unify several notions of expressivity under a single geometric quantity. The connection between trajectory length and transitions is intuitive and well supported by the figures. The dichotomy experiments are also interesting, particularly the observation that remaining depth, rather than absolute layer index alone, predicts the diversity of labelings obtained by sweeping a layer’s weights. The paper’s empirical figures are generally clear and convey the main message- that depth has a much stronger effect than width on the proposed measures in the tested regimes-very effectively. I also appreciate that the authors do not restrict attention to purely theoretical statements about untrained networks, but attempt to draw consequences for training behavior and layerwise influence.


The theorem on trajectory-length growth is potentially the most important contribution. If correct and stated with sufficient precision, it provides a useful mathematical explanation for the exponential depth dependence observed in the simulations. The paper’s broad structure is also compelling: first identify a mechanism in random networks, then relate it to several expressivity measures, then test implications for trained networks. This gives the work a coherent narrative and makes it more interesting than a collection of unrelated counting arguments.


That said, the current version has several weaknesses that limit the strength of the conclusions. The first is that many of the claims connecting trajectory length to expressivity are empirical or only partially formalized. The paper proves a lower bound on expected trajectory length growth for hard-tanh networks, and it proves a result on transitions in a large-weight limit. However, the broader claim that trajectory length is “responsible for” the depth sensitivity of transitions, activation patterns, and dichotomies is not fully established at the same level of rigor. The linear relationship between trajectory length and transitions is shown experimentally and argued intuitively although it would be helpful to have a clearer statement of the conditions under which this proportionality should hold. For example, how does the curvature or self-intersection structure of the latent trajectory affect the number of crossings? Could a long trajectory remain largely within one side of a readout hyperplane, or repeatedly traverse correlated regions, thereby reducing transitions? The paper’s intuition is plausible for random orientations, but the precise probabilistic assumptions should be made explicit.


A second concern is that the expressivity measures, while natural, are not always clearly tied to useful function approximation or task performance. Counting transitions along a one-dimensional path can reveal complexity along that path, but it is not obvious how this measure scales to high-dimensional data manifolds or how it relates to classification performance in realistic settings. Similarly, activation pattern counts can be enormous without necessarily implying useful representational structure. The dichotomy measure is closer to classification and yet the experimental setup involves sweeping weights along a one-dimensional great circle trajectory. This is a rather special subset of parameter space. The paper would benefit from a more careful discussion of what these measures do and do not capture. In particular, greater trajectory length may reflect instability or sensitivity rather than useful expressivity. The later training experiments seem to acknowledge this tension. This distinction should be made more central.


The empirical results on trained networks are intriguing but not yet fully convincing. The experiments in which only one layer is trained show that earlier layers perform better than later layers, consistent with the remaining-depth hypothesis. However, there are possible confounds. Training an earlier layer changes the representation that all subsequent random layers process, while training a later layer operates on a fixed random representation from lower layers. The observed effect may indeed be due to remaining depth. It could also depend on optimization conditioning, signal propagation, saturation patterns, or the compatibility of random upper layers with the learned lower representation. The paper should include additional controls. For instance, do the results persist for ReLU networks, for different initializations, for batch normalization or normalized activations, or when training comparable blocks rather than single layers? What happens if one trains a layer and the final readout together? Does the monotonic trend hold across multiple random seeds with confidence intervals? The figures suggest a pattern, but the paper should quantify variability.


The training trajectory-length experiments are also interesting but somewhat under-interpreted. For large weight variance, training decreases trajectory length. For smaller variance, training increases it. The authors describe this as a tradeoff between stability and expressivity, but the mechanism remains unclear. Is the change in trajectory length correlated with test accuracy, training loss, margin, or calibration? Is the decrease for large variance simply moving the network away from a chaotic or saturated regime into a more trainable regime? Does the increase for smaller variance occur mainly near the data manifold or globally? The paper compares MNIST interpolation curves and random input interpolation curves, which is useful, but more systematic measurements over many pairs of inputs and across different datasets would make the claim stronger.


The scope of the theoretical analysis is also somewhat narrow relative to the language used in the paper. Much of the formal work is for hard-tanh networks, with some results extended or stated for ReLU activation patterns. Hard-tanh networks are analytically convenient, but they are not the default architecture in most modern applications. The authors say that hard-tanh results carry over to tanh with additional technical steps. However, the relevance to ReLU networks, convolutional architectures, residual networks, and normalized networks is less clear. Since the paper’s motivation is modern deep learning, it would be valuable either to extend the main trajectory-length theorem to ReLU-like nonlinearities or to more explicitly delimit the conclusions. In ReLU networks, the absence of saturation and the possibility of unbounded activations may change the geometry of trajectory length substantially.


The presentation is generally readable, but some definitions and theorem statements need tightening. The theorem on trajectory-length growth uses big-O notation inside a lower bound, which is potentially confusing: a lower bound of the form “greater than O(·)” is not mathematically precise unless constants and asymptotic regimes are specified carefully. The statement that the bound is tight in the limits of large weight variance and width should be clarified. The assumptions on the input trajectory are also somewhat informal. The condition involving nonzero perpendicular magnitude should be stated precisely. Similarly, the activation-pattern upper bound is described as tight. The paper should clarify in what sense tightness is achieved and whether it holds for generic weights, worst-case weights, or some asymptotic construction. Since this paper relies heavily on geometric intuition, precise definitions are especially important.


I have several specific questions for the authors. First, can the proportionality between trajectory length and transition count be formalized for random readout hyperplanes under assumptions on the trajectory, perhaps in terms of expected crossings? Second, how sensitive are the empirical results to the choice of input curve? Circular interpolation between random vectors or data points is convenient, but would the same conclusions hold for more realistic paths on the data manifold or for random walks in input space? Third, in the single-layer training experiments, how many random seeds were used, and how large is the variance across seeds? Fourth, does the remaining-depth effect persist if the final linear classifier is trained in all conditions, or if the trained layer is initialized differently from the frozen layers? Fifth, can the authors clarify whether increased trajectory length is always desirable from an expressivity standpoint, or whether it should be interpreted as a capacity measure that may be harmful when excessive? Finally, how do the results change for ReLU networks, which are more commonly used and are already discussed in the activation-pattern section?


Overall, I view this as a creative and potentially significant paper with a compelling central idea, but the strongest claims need more careful qualification. The trajectory-length framework is promising and may provide a useful bridge between random network geometry, expressivity counts, and training behavior. The empirical evidence is suggestive, and the theoretical analysis is a valuable start. However, some of the links among the different expressivity measures remain informal, and the trained-network experiments are limited. In addition, the applicability beyond hard-tanh fully connected networks is not yet fully established. My recommendation is a weak accept. I think the paper would be a worthwhile contribution because it introduces a novel perspective and raises important questions, but it should be revised to state its claims more precisely, add controls for the training experiments, and better distinguish between expressivity as potential complexity and expressivity as useful task-relevant representation.
